title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
4.1 Missing values
The first move is to count missingness column by column, because the next decisions depend on how concentrated it is.


Show the code
raw |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "n_missing") |>
  filter(n_missing > 0) |>
  arrange(desc(n_missing)) |>
  kable(caption = "Variables with missing values")



Variables with missing values


variable
n_missing




fuel_type
1287


vehicle_value
513


vehicle_age
2


age_driving_licence
2





Missingness is concentrated in fuel_type (1,287 rows), vehicle_value (513 rows), and a handful of rows in vehicle_age and age_driving_licence. Together this is well under one percent of the data. Rather than impute, the safer move is to drop rows where any of these are missing, because vehicle value will be used for quintile binning and fuel type will be used as a grouping variable in bivariate analysis.


Show the code
ins <- raw |>
  drop_na(fuel_type, vehicle_value, vehicle_age, age_driving_licence)

nrow(ins)


[1] 352338


The working dataset retains 352,338 rows, which is 99.5 percent of the original.






NoteThings I learnt



Dropping is justifiable here because the missingness is small and the variables affected are central to later analysis. If fuel_type were missing in 10 percent of rows, an “unknown” category would be the right move instead, because dropping would silently bias the rest of the portfolio metrics.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
4.2 Recoding categorical variables
The cookbook codes carry information but lose interpretability the moment they hit a chart. The next step is to rewrite them as ordered factors, so axes and legends read naturally.


Show the code
ins <- ins |>
  mutate(
    policy_type = factor(
      policy_type,
      levels = c("TP", "TPG", "CC", "COMP_E", "COMP_N"),
      labels = c(
        "TP\nThird party only",
        "TPG\nThird party + glass",
        "CC\nThird party + 2 covers",
        "COMP_E\nComprehensive with excess",
        "COMP_N\nComprehensive no excess"
      )
    ),
    policy_status = factor(
      policy_status,
      levels = c("A", "C"),
      labels = c("Active", "Cancelled")
    ),
    business_type = factor(
      business_type,
      levels = c("NB", "P"),
      labels = c("New business", "Portfolio renewal")
    ),
    payment_frequency = factor(
      payment_frequency,
      levels = c("A", "S", "Q"),
      labels = c("Annual", "Semiannual", "Quarterly")
    ),
    bonus_score = factor(
      bonus_score,
      levels = c("G", "N", "B"),
      labels = c("Good history", "Neutral", "Bad history")
    ),
    fuel_type = factor(
      fuel_type,
      levels = c("D", "G"),
      labels = c("Diesel", "Gasoline")
    ),
    municipality_type = factor(
      municipality_type,
      levels = c("I", "C", "IS"),
      labels = c("Inland", "Coastal", "Islands")
    ),
    circulation_area = factor(
      circulation_area,
      levels = c("U", "R"),
      labels = c("Urban", "Rural")
    ),
    year = factor(year)
  )








NoteThings I learnt



Two design choices sit behind the recoding above. The labels use newline characters so that the long descriptive label sits under a short code, keeping x axis ticks readable without rotation. The factor levels are ordered by coverage breadth, from minimal third party to comprehensive without excess, so the eye reads the chart from least to most insurance protection rather than alphabetically.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
4.3 Derived variables
Three derived variables make the analysis cleaner and more direct.
The first is licence tenure. The cookbook flags that age_driving_licence is the calendar year of licence issuance, not the number of years held. Subtracting it from the policy year gives the actual experience behind the wheel, which is the more relevant underwriting signal.


Show the code
ins <- ins |>
  mutate(
    licence_tenure = as.numeric(as.character(year)) - age_driving_licence,
    licence_tenure = pmax(licence_tenure, 0)
  )

summary(ins$licence_tenure)


   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
   1944    1998    2003    2000    2004    2024 


The pmax floor handles two records where the subtraction produced a small negative number, which would be impossible by construction. They are coded as zero years of tenure, which is the most conservative interpretation.
The second is age bins for the driver, in five year intervals between 18 and 75, plus an over 75 group. The bins follow common actuarial conventions and align cleanly with how risk is generally priced.


Show the code
ins <- ins |>
  mutate(
    age_band = cut(
      driver_age,
      breaks = c(17, 25, 35, 45, 55, 65, 75, 99),
      labels = c("18-25", "26-35", "36-45", "46-55", "56-65", "66-75", "76+"),
      ordered_result = TRUE
    )
  )


The third is vehicle value quintile, computed with ntile. Quintiles control for the heavy right skew of vehicle value, where the top percent stretches from 71,000 EUR to over 374,000 EUR.


Show the code
ins <- ins |>
  mutate(
    value_quintile = ntile(vehicle_value, 5),
    value_quintile = factor(
      value_quintile,
      levels = 1:5,
      labels = c("Q1\nLowest", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4", "Q5\nHighest")
    )
  )

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 5.1 Target variables: premium and incurred amount
text:
5.1 Target variables: premium and incurred amount


Show the code
theme_va <- function() {
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 10),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      axis.title.y = element_text(angle = 90),
      plot.background = element_rect(fill = "#fafafa", colour = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#fafafa", colour = NA)
    )
}

acc <- "#0072B2"
warm <- "#D55E00"
neutral <- "#999999"

mean_prem <- mean(ins$total_premium)
median_prem <- median(ins$total_premium)

box_prem <- ggplot(ins, aes(x = total_premium, y = "")) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = neutral, colour = "grey30", outlier.alpha = 0.05, outlier.size = 0.6) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", colour = warm, size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"), limits = c(0, 1500)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_blank())

hist_prem <- ggplot(ins, aes(x = total_premium)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = acc, alpha = 0.85, colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = median_prem, colour = "grey20", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean_prem, colour = warm, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = median_prem + 35, y = 38000,
           label = paste0("Median \u20ac", round(median_prem)),
           colour = "grey20", hjust = 0, size = 3.4) +
  annotate("text", x = mean_prem + 35, y = 34000,
           label = paste0("Mean \u20ac", round(mean_prem)),
           colour = warm, hjust = 0, size = 3.4) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"), limits = c(0, 1500)) +
  labs(
    title = "Total premium per policy year",
    subtitle = "Heavy right skew. Most policies pay under \u20ac400, a thin tail reaches above \u20ac1,500.",
    x = "Total premium",
    y = "Number of policy years"
  ) +
  theme_va()

# Incurred is more extreme: zero-inflated and heavy right tail
inc_nonzero <- ins |> filter(total_incurred > 0)
mean_inc <- mean(inc_nonzero$total_incurred)
median_inc <- median(inc_nonzero$total_incurred)

hist_inc <- ggplot(inc_nonzero, aes(x = total_incurred)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = warm, alpha = 0.85, colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = median_inc, colour = "grey20", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean_inc, colour = acc, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = median_inc + 100, y = 7500,
           label = paste0("Median \u20ac", round(median_inc)),
           colour = "grey20", hjust = 0, size = 3.4) +
  annotate("text", x = mean_inc + 100, y = 6800,
           label = paste0("Mean \u20ac", round(mean_inc)),
           colour = acc, hjust = 0, size = 3.4) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"), limits = c(0, 6000)) +
  labs(
    title = "Total incurred per policy year (policies with at least one claim)",
    subtitle = paste0("Among the ",
                      scales::comma(nrow(inc_nonzero)),
                      " policy years with claims, the mean is pulled well above the median by the right tail."),
    x = "Total incurred",
    y = "Number of policy years"
  ) +
  theme_va()

(box_prem / hist_prem / hist_inc) +
  plot_layout(heights = c(0.4, 1, 1))





Distributions of total premium and total incurred amount. The boxplot above each histogram summarises the same data in a different idiom, making the right skew visible at a glance.










TipReading the plot



The premium distribution is right skewed but reasonably concentrated, with most policies paying between 150 EUR and 400 EUR. The mean of 296 EUR sits well above the median of 258 EUR, which is the classic signature of a right tail. The incurred distribution is more extreme. 86 percent of policy years pay no claim at all and are not even plotted. Among the 14 percent that do claim, the mean is roughly 1,750 EUR but the median is under 1,000 EUR, with a thin tail running well past 5,000 EUR and an absolute maximum above 570,000 EUR. The takeaway for the rest of this report is that any chart of incurred amounts must respect this zero inflation and heavy tail. A naive histogram of all 354,000 incurred values would show a single spike at zero and tell us nothing.








NoteThings I learnt



Pairing a boxplot above a histogram is one of the cleanest ways to read a right skewed distribution. The boxplot makes the median, the IQR, and the outliers immediately legible, and the histogram fills in the shape between them. The two views share an x axis on patchwork, which is the entire reason for using patchwork here rather than facet_wrap. The two panels show the same variable in two complementary idioms, not two slices of the data.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 5.2 Driver age, vehicle age, and licence tenure
text:
5.2 Driver age, vehicle age, and licence tenure
These three numerical variables are the core underwriting inputs in motor insurance, so they get a comparative panel.


Show the code
make_density <- function(var, title, fill_col) {
  ggplot(ins, aes(x = .data[[var]])) +
    geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                   bins = 30, fill = fill_col, alpha = 0.85, colour = "white") +
    geom_density(colour = "grey20", linewidth = 0.5) +
    labs(title = title, x = "Years", y = NULL) +
    theme_va() +
    theme(axis.text.y = element_blank())
}

p_driver <- make_density("driver_age", "Driver age", acc)
p_vehicle <- make_density("vehicle_age", "Vehicle age", warm)
p_licence <- make_density("licence_tenure", "Licence tenure", "#009E73")

p_driver + p_vehicle + p_licence





Distributions of driver age, vehicle age, and licence tenure. The shared scale on the x axis is intentional because all three are measured in years.










TipReading the plot



Driver age peaks in the mid forties and is roughly symmetric, with very few drivers under 25 and a thin tail past 75. Vehicle age is bimodal in a soft way, with one mode around fifteen years and another around twenty five, suggesting the portfolio mixes mid life cars with a substantial population of older vehicles still being insured. Licence tenure is the most informative of the three, peaking strongly around twenty years and falling away on both sides. The right tail reflects retired or near retired drivers who held their licence for decades, while the left side is sparsely populated, which is consistent with a small share of recently licensed drivers in the book.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
5.3 Categorical variables
The portfolio descriptors get a faceted bar chart so they can be read together. Bars are ordered by inherent meaning where one exists (coverage breadth, bonus score) and by frequency otherwise.


Show the code
make_bar <- function(var, title) {
  ins |>
    count(.data[[var]]) |>
    mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = .data[[var]], y = n)) +
    geom_col(fill = acc, alpha = 0.85) +
    geom_text(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)),
              vjust = -0.4, size = 3, colour = "grey20") +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)), labels = comma) +
    labs(title = title, x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_va() +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8))
}

p1 <- make_bar("year", "Policy year")
p2 <- make_bar("policy_type", "Policy type") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7))
p3 <- make_bar("policy_status", "Policy status")
p4 <- make_bar("business_type", "Business origin")
p5 <- make_bar("payment_frequency", "Payment frequency")
p6 <- make_bar("bonus_score", "Bonus score")
p7 <- make_bar("fuel_type", "Fuel type")
p8 <- make_bar("circulation_area", "Circulation area")

(p1 + p2 + p3) /
(p4 + p5 + p6) /
(p7 + p8 + plot_spacer())





Composition of the portfolio across eight categorical descriptors. Percentages are calculated within each panel.










TipReading the plot



The book is growing rapidly. 2024 contributes 47 percent of all policy years, up from 19 percent in 2022, so almost half of everything in this dataset is recent. CC (third party plus two additional covers) is the dominant product at 58 percent, followed by comprehensive with excess at 26 percent. Comprehensive without excess (COMP_N) and basic third party (TP) are the smallest segments. The portfolio is heavily skewed toward annual paying customers with a good bonus history, on diesel vehicles, in inland municipalities. The 11 percent cancellation share is something to keep an eye on, because cancelled policies often signal claims related friction.








NoteThings I learnt



A small detail in the bar chart matters: the y axis scale uses expand to push the upper bound 15 percent above the tallest bar so the percentage labels do not collide with the panel border. This is a tiny adjustment but it is the difference between a chart that looks rushed and one that looks intentional. Direct labels also remove the need for a y axis altogether in principle, which honours the data ink ratio (the share of plot ink that conveys data rather than decoration). The axis is kept here only because the panel shares its style with bars used later that lack direct labels.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
5.4 Vehicle brand: long tail categorical
Vehicle brand has 68 distinct values, which exceeds what a bar chart can show legibly. The standard move is to keep the top N and group the rest as “other”, then sort by frequency. Showing 68 bars unsorted would be unreadable.


Show the code
brand_tbl <- ins |>
  count(vehicle_brand, sort = TRUE) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))

top15 <- head(brand_tbl, 15)
other_total <- sum(brand_tbl$n[-(1:15)])
other_pct <- other_total / sum(brand_tbl$n)

top15_plot <- bind_rows(
  top15,
  tibble(vehicle_brand = "Other (53 brands)", n = other_total, pct = other_pct)
) |>
  mutate(vehicle_brand = fct_reorder(vehicle_brand, n))

ggplot(top15_plot, aes(x = n, y = vehicle_brand)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = vehicle_brand == "Other (53 brands)"), alpha = 0.85, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(comma(n), " (", percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1), ")")),
            hjust = -0.05, size = 3.2, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = acc, "TRUE" = neutral)) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18)), labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "The brand mix is concentrated in volume European marques",
    subtitle = "Top 15 brands account for 92 percent of all policy years",
    x = "Number of policy years",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())





Top 15 vehicle brands by policy year count. The remaining 53 brands together account for 8 percent of the portfolio.










TipReading the plot



The seven largest brands are all volume European marques, with Renault, Citroen, and Peugeot leading. Premium German marques such as Audi, Mercedes, and BMW appear next, which is relevant because these brands carry higher vehicle values and higher repair costs. The shape of this distribution is what statisticians call long tail. A handful of brands dominate, and the remaining 53 brands sit in a thin tail that contributes 8 percent of the book.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
6.1 Year on year deterioration
The first bivariate question is the temporal one. Has the portfolio held its profitability over the three years, or is something moving?


Show the code
yearly <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(year)

p_lr <- ggplot(yearly, aes(x = year, y = loss_ratio)) +
  geom_col(fill = warm, alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, colour = "grey50", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Loss ratio", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_va()

p_freq <- ggplot(yearly, aes(x = year, y = claim_freq)) +
  geom_col(fill = acc, alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = number(claim_freq, accuracy = 0.001)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Claim frequency", subtitle = "Claims per unit of exposure", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_va()

p_prem <- ggplot(yearly, aes(x = year, y = avg_premium)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#009E73", alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollar(avg_premium, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Average premium per exposure", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_va()

p_lr + p_freq + p_prem





Aggregate portfolio metrics by policy year. Both loss ratio and average premium have risen in 2024.










TipReading the plot



Loss ratio rose from 66 percent in 2022 to 75 percent in 2024, a 14 percent relative increase. Claim frequency drifted up only slightly, from 0.303 to 0.312, which is essentially flat. Average premium per exposure moved in the wrong direction, falling from 446 EUR to 404 EUR over the three years. The arithmetic conclusion is unavoidable: pricing is softening while claim cost holds steady, and the loss ratio is the result. This single chart sets the agenda for everything that follows. The question is no longer whether the book is deteriorating, but which segments are pulling the loss ratio up.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
6.2 Loss ratio by policy type
Policy type is the most informative segmentation in motor insurance because it determines the coverage breadth and therefore the universe of claims the insurer pays.


Show the code
by_type <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(policy_type)

ggplot(by_type, aes(x = policy_type, y = loss_ratio)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = loss_ratio > 0.7), alpha = 0.85, width = 0.65, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.7, colour = "grey50", linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.6, y = 0.72, label = "70 percent target",
           colour = "grey40", size = 3, hjust = 0) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#009E73", "TRUE" = warm)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)),
                     limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Comprehensive without excess is the loss-ratio outlier",
    subtitle = "Loss ratio aggregated as total incurred over total premium across all three years",
    x = NULL, y = "Loss ratio"
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8))





Loss ratio by policy type, with average premium overlaid. COMP_N runs a markedly higher loss ratio despite charging the highest premium.










TipReading the plot



The five policy types fall into three bands. TP (third party only) and CC (third party plus two additional covers) sit in the green zone, comfortably under the 70 percent reference line. TPG (third party plus glass) sits just under at 69 percent. COMP_E (comprehensive with excess) crosses the line at 74 percent. COMP_N (comprehensive without excess) sits in its own band at 94 percent, very close to the underwriting break even point before expenses are even loaded. The pattern is consistent with insurance theory: removing the excess transfers all small claim cost from the policyholder to the insurer, so claim frequency should rise sharply. The next chart confirms whether that is what is happening.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 6.3 Decomposing the loss ratio into frequency and severity
text:
6.3 Decomposing the loss ratio into frequency and severity
Loss ratio equals claim frequency multiplied by claim severity divided by average premium. Plotting frequency and severity side by side reveals which lever is pulling.


Show the code
by_type_long <- by_type |>
  select(policy_type, claim_freq, severity) |>
  pivot_longer(c(claim_freq, severity), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  mutate(metric = recode(metric,
                         claim_freq = "Claim frequency (claims per exposure)",
                         severity = "Severity (mean incurred per claim)"))

ggplot(by_type_long, aes(x = policy_type, y = value, fill = metric)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(metric == "Claim frequency (claims per exposure)",
                               number(value, accuracy = 0.01),
                               dollar(value, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1))),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(acc, warm)) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  facet_wrap(~ metric, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL,
       title = "Frequency, not severity, drives the COMP_N problem") +
  theme_va() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7),
        strip.text = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"))





Frequency-severity decomposition by policy type. COMP_N has 5x the frequency of TP, while severity is comparable across types.










TipReading the plot



Severity is remarkably stable across policy types, sitting between 750 EUR and 1,100 EUR per claim. The cost of an individual claim does not change much with the product. Frequency, however, varies by a factor of nearly eight, from 0.13 claims per exposure on TP to 1.05 on COMP_N. COMP_N policyholders are not having worse accidents. They are simply claiming for everything, because there is no excess to absorb small losses. The pricing implication is clear: COMP_N should either carry a much higher loading or reintroduce an excess to align incentives with the rest of the book.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 6.4 How the deterioration is concentrated by policy type and year
text:
6.4 How the deterioration is concentrated by policy type and year
The previous two charts looked at aggregates over three years. The next chart asks whether the loss ratio uplift in 2024 is broad or concentrated.


Show the code
by_type_year <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(policy_type, year)

ggplot(by_type_year, aes(x = year, y = loss_ratio,
                          group = policy_type, colour = policy_type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = filter(by_type_year, year == "2024"),
    aes(label = percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 1)),
    nudge_x = 0.15, direction = "y", size = 3.2, hjust = 0,
    segment.alpha = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, colour = "grey60", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  expand_limits(x = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Loss ratio trajectories diverge after 2022",
    subtitle = "COMP_N crossed 100 percent in 2024",
    x = NULL, y = "Loss ratio", colour = "Policy type"
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7)
  )





Loss ratio by policy type and year. The deterioration is broadly distributed, but COMP_N crossed the underwriting break-even point in 2024.










TipReading the plot



Every policy type except CC is moving up. COMP_N rose from 73 percent to 102 percent in two years, and is the only line that crosses the underwriting break even point. COMP_E and TPG climbed in step from 60 to 75 percent. TP (the smallest book) more than doubled from 30 percent to 65 percent. CC, the largest book, oscillated around 60 percent and is the only segment holding steady. The implication is that the portfolio has three distinct problem children with different trajectories. A blanket rate increase would over charge CC customers who are not the source of the trouble.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 6.5 Bonus score and the pricing versus experience gap
text:
6.5 Bonus score and the pricing versus experience gap
Bonus score is a proxy for the insurer’s prior view of the policyholder, set at underwriting. A policyholder with a bad claims history pays a higher premium, but the question is whether the loading is sufficient.


Show the code
by_bonus <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(bonus_score)

p_prem <- ggplot(by_bonus, aes(x = bonus_score, y = avg_premium)) +
  geom_col(fill = acc, alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollar(avg_premium, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Average premium per exposure", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_va()

p_lr <- ggplot(by_bonus, aes(x = bonus_score, y = loss_ratio)) +
  geom_col(fill = warm, alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.7, colour = "grey50", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Loss ratio", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_va()

p_prem + p_lr





Average premium and loss ratio by bonus score. Bad-history policyholders pay 2.2x more in premium yet still produce the highest loss ratio.










TipReading the plot



Pricing tiers in a textbook bonus malus system should produce the opposite of what this chart shows. Good history drivers should run a low loss ratio at low premium, neutral drivers should sit roughly at break even, and bad history drivers should run a low loss ratio at high premium because the loading more than offsets their elevated risk. What the data shows instead is a U shape on loss ratio, with good drivers at 71 percent and bad drivers at 85 percent. The bad history loading of 2.2x is not pulling its weight. Either the bad history segment is being under priced or the loading is correctly calibrated and the segment has worsened over time. A look at the year on year breakdown helps choose between the two explanations.




Show the code
by_bonus_year <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(bonus_score, year)

ggplot(by_bonus_year, aes(x = year, y = loss_ratio,
                          group = bonus_score, colour = bonus_score)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, colour = "grey60", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Good history" = "#009E73",
                                  "Neutral" = neutral,
                                  "Bad history" = warm)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Bad-history policies deteriorated sharply",
    subtitle = "53 percent loss ratio in 2022 became 93 percent in 2024",
    x = NULL, y = "Loss ratio", colour = "Bonus score"
  ) +
  theme_va()





Loss ratio by bonus score and year. The bad-history loss ratio almost doubled from 2022 to 2024.










TipReading the plot



Bad history policies climbed from 53 percent to 93 percent loss ratio in two years, and the neutral segment overshot bad history briefly in 2024 at 101 percent. Good history policies climbed too but stayed inside acceptable territory. The combination tells a clearer story: against a backdrop of softening premium across the whole book, the loadings for the risky segments have not kept pace with experience. The pricing model needs the bad and neutral tiers recalibrated using recent claim data, not just a uniform rate increase.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 6.6 Driver age and the loss ratio inversion
text:
6.6 Driver age and the loss ratio inversion
Driver age in motor insurance follows a well established U shape: youngest and oldest drivers have the highest risk, middle aged drivers the lowest. The question is whether this book follows the textbook pattern, and whether pricing reflects it.


Show the code
by_age <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(age_band)

# Two y axes are reasonable here because both are continuous and meaningfully connected to the x axis
sc <- max(by_age$avg_premium) / max(by_age$loss_ratio)

ggplot(by_age, aes(x = age_band)) +
  geom_col(aes(y = loss_ratio), fill = warm, alpha = 0.75, width = 0.7) +
  geom_line(aes(y = avg_premium / sc, group = 1), colour = acc, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(y = avg_premium / sc), colour = acc, size = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(y = loss_ratio, label = percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.2, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_text(aes(y = avg_premium / sc,
                label = dollar(avg_premium, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -1.0, size = 3.2, colour = acc) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.7, colour = "grey50", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Loss ratio",
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . * sc,
                        name = "Average premium per exposure",
                        labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Pricing falls as drivers age, but loss ratio spikes again at 76+",
    subtitle = "The 18-25 band is correctly loaded. The 76+ band is not.",
    x = NULL
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(
    axis.title.y.left = element_text(colour = warm),
    axis.title.y.right = element_text(colour = acc)
  )





Loss ratio and average premium by driver age band. The 76+ band shows a sharp loss ratio spike that pricing does not anticipate.










TipReading the plot



The textbook U shape on loss ratio shows up clearly, with 18-25 at 63 percent, the middle bands hovering between 68 and 73 percent, and 76+ jumping to 102 percent. The average premium curve does part of the right thing. It loads the 18-25 band correctly at 887 EUR, well above the portfolio average. However, the loading flattens after that, and at the over 75 end pricing actually drops back to 390 EUR, below the average. The pricing model treats older drivers as low risk when the data says they have crossed the underwriting break even point. The 76+ segment is small (about 0.8 percent of the book), so the financial impact is limited, but it is a clear signal that the senior driver tariff needs review.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
6.7 Statistical validation with Kruskal Wallis
The visual differences across groups raise an obvious question. Could the differences be due to chance? The distribution of incurred amounts per policy is heavy tailed and far from normal, so a parametric ANOVA would mislead. The Kruskal Wallis test is the nonparametric equivalent and is robust to non normality. The ggstatsplot package puts the statistical annotation directly on the plot, which means the test result and the visual evidence sit together for the reader.


Show the code
# Work with claims-only subset to avoid the log(0) trap, and downsample for ggstatsplot performance
set.seed(608)
claims_sample <- ins |>
  filter(total_incurred > 0) |>
  slice_sample(n = 5000)

ggbetweenstats(
  data = claims_sample,
  x = policy_type,
  y = total_incurred,
  type = "nonparametric",
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  centrality.point.args = list(size = 4, color = warm),
  violin.args = list(width = 0.7, alpha = 0.4),
  title = "Incurred per claiming policy differs significantly across policy types",
  xlab = NULL,
  ylab = "Total incurred (log scale)"
) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac")) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7))





Distribution of log incurred (for policies with claims) by policy type, with Kruskal-Wallis test annotation. The test confirms that the differences in incurred amount across policy types are statistically significant.










TipReading the plot



The Kruskal Wallis chi squared statistic is large and the p value is effectively zero, confirming that incurred amounts are not drawn from the same distribution across policy types. The pairwise comparisons (only significant ones shown) attach numerical evidence to what the eye already sees. COMP_E and COMP_N concentrate around larger claim amounts than the third party variants, with median incurred about double. The log scale on the y axis is essential here because the raw incurred distribution would crush all the structure into the bottom decile of the plot.








NoteThings I learnt



ggstatsplot is doing two jobs in one chart: it draws the violin and the boxplot in a single composite, and it runs the Kruskal Wallis test plus pairwise post hoc comparisons and writes the results into the subtitle. The principle in play is that the test belongs on the plot. A reader who sees a difference between groups will ask “is it significant?” within a second of seeing the visual, and the conventional answer (a separate table on the next page) breaks the reader’s flow. Embedding the statistic where the question forms is the move.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 6.8 Statistical validation: claim frequency by payment frequency
text:
6.8 Statistical validation: claim frequency by payment frequency
The previous test compared continuous incurred amounts. Claim frequency is a count rate, and the question of whether it differs by payment frequency is a categorical on count problem. A chi square test on a 2x2 contingency of “claim or not” by payment frequency answers it.


Show the code
ins_test <- ins |>
  mutate(any_claim = total_claims > 0)

ggbarstats(
  data = ins_test,
  x = any_claim,
  y = payment_frequency,
  type = "parametric",
  bf.message = FALSE,
  proportion.test = TRUE,
  label = "percentage",
  title = "Claim probability is higher for quarterly payers",
  xlab = NULL,
  ylab = "Share of policies",
  legend.title = "At least one claim"
) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = warm, "FALSE" = "#a6cee3")) +
  theme_va()





Share of policies with at least one claim, by payment frequency. The chi-square test confirms quarterly payers claim significantly more often.










TipReading the plot



The chi square statistic is highly significant. Quarterly payers are about 30 percent more likely to file at least one claim than annual payers. This is a classic underwriting signal: payment frequency is correlated with cash flow stress, and policyholders under cash flow stress claim differently. Pricing the quarterly tier higher is already standard practice in most insurers, and this chart shows the loading is justified, though whether it is large enough is a separate question that the loss ratio chart for payment frequency would answer.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 7.1 Heatmap: loss ratio by age band and policy type
text:
7.1 Heatmap: loss ratio by age band and policy type


Show the code
hm <- ins |>
  portfolio_metrics(age_band, policy_type) |>
  mutate(reliable = policies >= 200)

ggplot(hm, aes(x = policy_type, y = age_band, fill = loss_ratio)) +
  geom_tile(aes(alpha = reliable), colour = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(reliable, percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 1), "")),
            size = 3, colour = "grey15") +
  scale_fill_continuous_diverging(palette = "Blue-Red 3", mid = 0.7,
                                    name = "Loss ratio", labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_alpha_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = 1, `FALSE` = 0.2), guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Loss ratio runs hottest for middle-aged drivers on comprehensive cover",
    subtitle = "Diverging palette centred at the 70 percent profitability threshold",
    x = NULL, y = "Driver age band"
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7),
    legend.position = "right"
  )





Loss ratio heatmap with cells annotated. White cells indicate fewer than 200 policies, where the ratio is too noisy to read.










TipReading the plot



The hottest cells are the comprehensive products at every middle age band, with several crossing 90 percent. The 76+ row is consistently red across products, with CC reaching 128 percent. The 18-25 row, in contrast, is mostly white or blue, suggesting that despite the high per policy premium the segment is correctly underwritten. The colour scale is diverging at 70 percent rather than at zero, because the meaningful break for a P&C insurer is the loss ratio threshold above which the line stops being profitable, not the mathematical zero. Centering the divergence at the analytically meaningful value is a small choice that completely changes how the chart reads.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 7.2 Ridge plot: incurred distribution by year and bonus score
text:
7.2 Ridge plot: incurred distribution by year and bonus score
The previous heatmap showed central tendencies. The next chart shows whole distributions, which is where the tail behaviour and volatility surface.


Show the code
ridge_data <- ins |>
  filter(total_incurred > 0)

ggplot(ridge_data, aes(x = total_incurred, y = bonus_score, fill = bonus_score)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.7, scale = 0.95, quantile_lines = TRUE,
                      quantiles = c(0.5)) +
  facet_wrap(~ year, ncol = 3) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Good history" = "#009E73",
                                "Neutral" = neutral,
                                "Bad history" = warm)) +
  labs(
    title = "Incurred amounts grow heavier-tailed for bad-history policies",
    subtitle = "Vertical line marks the median",
    x = "Total incurred (log scale)", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10))





Distribution of log incurred for claiming policies, by bonus score and year. The right tail is heavy across all groups but most pronounced for bad-history policies.










TipReading the plot



Three observations register. First, the median (the vertical line inside each ridge) drifts slightly to the right over time, but the movement is modest, consistent with the small severity per exposure rise observed in the year on year chart. Second, bad history policies sit at a higher median across all three years, as expected. Third, the right tails (anything above 5,000 EUR on the log axis) are heaviest for bad history policies in 2024, with a small visible bulge above 50,000 EUR that is absent in 2022. Volatility is concentrated in the bad history right tail. This matters for capital allocation: the rare big claim is what consumes reserves, and the bad history book has more of them.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 7.3 Faceted scatter: severity vs frequency by policy type
text:
7.3 Faceted scatter: severity vs frequency by policy type
A bubble chart with frequency on the x axis, severity on the y axis, and bubble size proportional to premium volume is the canonical actuarial diagnostic for understanding which segments earn what they need to earn.


Show the code
fs <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(policy_type, year)

ggplot(fs, aes(x = claim_freq, y = severity, size = premium, colour = policy_type)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = paste0(policy_type, " ", year)),
                  size = 2.6, max.overlaps = 20, segment.alpha = 0.3) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 14), labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"),
                         name = "Premium volume") +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1", guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Claim frequency (claims per exposure)") +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Severity (incurred per claim)",
                     labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac")) +
  labs(
    title = "COMP_N occupies its own quadrant: high frequency, low severity",
    subtitle = "Bubble area is proportional to premium volume"
  ) +
  theme_va()





Severity-frequency-volume map by policy type and year. Distance from the origin reflects expected loss cost per exposure.










TipReading the plot



COMP_N forms a tight cluster on the far right at high frequency and low severity, the signature of a deductible free product. TP sits on the bottom left at low frequency and low severity, as it should given its narrow coverage. CC, the largest premium volume on the chart, sits in the lower middle and is the workhorse. COMP_E moves up and to the right between 2022 and 2024, a worrying trajectory: both frequency and severity are rising for the comprehensive with excess product. The diagnostic value of this chart is that distance from the origin equals expected loss cost per exposure, so a point in the upper right is structurally more expensive to insure regardless of how the loss ratio currently reads.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 8.1 Where the loss is concentrated by coverage line
text:
8.1 Where the loss is concentrated by coverage line
The total premium has eight coverage components. Decomposing the total loss ratio into per coverage loss ratios reveals which lines are profitable and which are loss making within each product.


Show the code
coverages <- tribble(
  ~coverage, ~premium_col, ~incurred_col,
  "Liability", "liability_premium", "liability_incurred",
  "Property damage", "property_damage_premium", "property_incurred",
  "Glass", "glass_premium", "glass_incurred",
  "Occupants", "occupants_premium", "occupants_incurred",
  "Fire", "fire_premium", "fire_incurred",
  "Theft", "theft_premium", "theft_incurred",
  "Legal protection", "legal_protection_premium", "legal_protection_incurred"
)

coverage_lr <- coverages |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    premium = sum(ins[[premium_col]], na.rm = TRUE),
    incurred = sum(ins[[incurred_col]], na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    loss_ratio = incurred / premium,
    coverage = fct_reorder(coverage, premium)
  )

ggplot(coverage_lr, aes(x = loss_ratio, y = coverage)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = loss_ratio > 0.7), alpha = 0.85, width = 0.65, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1), "   (",
                                dollar(premium / 1e6, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 0.1),
                                "M premium)")),
            hjust = -0.05, size = 3.2, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.7, colour = "grey50", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#009E73", "TRUE" = warm)) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.35)),
                     limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Liability and property damage are the loss-making coverages",
    subtitle = "Theft, fire, legal, and occupants run comfortably under the 70 percent threshold",
    x = "Loss ratio", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_va()





Loss ratio by coverage line. Liability and property damage carry the load and run hot.










TipReading the plot



Liability premium is the largest line at 64 million EUR and runs a loss ratio of 74 percent. Property damage is the second largest at 26 million and runs 72 percent. Glass at 7 million runs 65 percent, just under threshold. The other four lines run between 40 and 52 percent and are clearly profitable. The arithmetic is unforgiving: the two largest coverages are loss making, and the four profitable smaller coverages are not large enough to cross subsidise them. Any portfolio rebuild has to start with liability and property damage.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
8.2 The injury claim severity problem
Within liability, the cookbook splits claims into property damage and bodily injury. Bodily injury is what costs.


Show the code
sev_decomp <- tribble(
  ~coverage,                  ~claims_col,                ~incurred_col,
  "Liability injury",         "liability_injury_claims",  "liability_injury_incurred",
  "Liability property",       "liability_property_claims","liability_property_incurred",
  "Property damage",          "property_claims",          "property_incurred",
  "Theft",                    "theft_claims",             "theft_incurred",
  "Glass",                    "glass_claims",             "glass_incurred",
  "Fire",                     "fire_claims",              "fire_incurred",
  "Occupants",                "occupants_claims",         "occupants_incurred",
  "Legal protection",         "legal_protection_claims",  "legal_protection_incurred"
) |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    total_claims = sum(ins[[claims_col]], na.rm = TRUE),
    total_incurred = sum(ins[[incurred_col]], na.rm = TRUE),
    severity = total_incurred / total_claims
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(coverage = fct_reorder(coverage, severity))

ggplot(sev_decomp, aes(x = severity, y = coverage)) +
  geom_col(fill = warm, alpha = 0.85, width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(dollar(severity, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1),
                                "  (", comma(total_claims), " claims)")),
            hjust = -0.05, size = 3.2, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.35))) +
  labs(
    title = "Bodily injury severity dwarfs every other claim type",
    subtitle = "One bodily injury claim costs the same as seven property damage claims",
    x = "Mean incurred per claim", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_va()





Mean incurred per claim by sub-coverage, for policies with at least one claim in that sub-coverage. Bodily injury claims average roughly 5x the cost of property damage claims.










TipReading the plot



A bodily injury claim costs roughly 4,940 EUR on average, more than four times the next largest category. Property damage averages 706 EUR. Glass averages well under 200 EUR. Bodily injury is rare (around 4,800 claims out of 50,500 total claims, or 9 percent) but every one of them is structurally more expensive. The implication for pricing is that the bodily injury sub tariff needs to track external indicators of medical and legal cost inflation, because that is where most of the recent loss ratio uplift in the liability line is coming from.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 8.3 Volatility: the coefficient of variation by segment
text:
8.3 Volatility: the coefficient of variation by segment
The coefficient of variation (the standard deviation divided by the mean of incurred per policy) is a clean measure of how risky each segment is for capital purposes. A CV of one means typical claim cost equals the average. A CV of fifteen means the typical large loss is fifteen times the average and capital is dominated by tail risk.


Show the code
cv_data <- ins |>
  group_by(policy_type, bonus_score) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    cv = sd(total_incurred) / mean(total_incurred),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n >= 200)

ggplot(cv_data, aes(x = policy_type, y = bonus_score, fill = cv)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = number(cv, accuracy = 0.1)), size = 3.2, colour = "grey15") +
  scale_fill_continuous_sequential(palette = "Reds 3", name = "CV") +
  labs(
    title = "TPG and CC under good-history cover carry the highest tail risk",
    subtitle = "CV is standard deviation of incurred divided by mean, computed per cell",
    x = NULL, y = "Bonus score"
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7))





Coefficient of variation of incurred per policy by policy type and bonus score. Higher values mean more capital required against tail losses.










TipReading the plot



The pattern inverts the loss ratio result. COMP_N, which has the worst loss ratio, has the lowest CV because every policyholder claims something and the distribution of claims is denser and less skewed. TPG and CC under good history cover, by contrast, have very high CV, between 14 and 18, because the typical policy has no claim at all and the rare loss is large. The takeaway for capital management is that the two product lines that look healthy on a loss ratio basis are actually where most of the reserve risk sits. Profitability and volatility are different problems and demand different responses. The CC book needs reinsurance attention even if its loss ratio is fine.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
10.1 Profitability
The portfolio loss ratio rose from 66 percent in 2022 to 75 percent in 2024, a 12 percent relative deterioration in two years. The deterioration is broad but unevenly distributed.
The headline driver is premium softening, not claims inflation. Claim frequency rose only 3 percent over the period and severity per exposure rose only 3 percent, both essentially flat. Average premium per exposure, however, fell roughly 9 percent over the three years, from 446 EUR to 404 EUR. The arithmetic is straightforward: with incurred costs flat and premium falling, loss ratio must rise. The softening is broad based across every policy type, and the business mix shifted from heavily new business in 2022 (97 percent of policies) toward renewals in 2024 (48 percent), both consistent with competitive pressure on rates. Within that broad pattern, two specific segments deserve attention: the two largest premium lines, liability at 64 million EUR and property damage at 26 million EUR, both run loss ratios in the low seventies, and within liability the bodily injury sub line carries a mean severity of 4,940 EUR per claim, the highest in the book. The four smaller lines (theft, fire, legal protection, occupants) run 40 to 52 percent and remain profitable.
By product, comprehensive without excess (COMP_N) crossed the underwriting break even point in 2024 at 102 percent loss ratio. The mechanism is structural: removing the excess multiplies claim frequency by a factor of five compared to basic third party, and pricing only partly compensates. Bad history policies almost doubled their loss ratio from 53 percent in 2022 to 93 percent in 2024, indicating that the bonus malus loading on the riskier tiers is stale.
By driver age, the over 75 segment runs a 102 percent loss ratio despite paying below average premium, which is a clear pricing mistake on a small but identifiable segment.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
10.2 Volatility
Loss ratio measures the average outcome, but the capital the insurer must hold is driven by the dispersion around the average. Volatility, measured as the coefficient of variation of incurred per policy, inverts the loss ratio ranking. The products that look healthy on a loss ratio basis (TPG and CC under good history cover) carry coefficients of variation between 14 and 18, meaning the typical large loss is many multiples of the average. COMP_N, in contrast, has a CV of 3.6 because most policyholders claim something and the right tail is thinner.
The bad history segment is volatile and loss making, which is the worst combination. The good history CC book is profitable on average but volatile, which is a different problem requiring reinsurance attention rather than a pricing fix.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
10.3 Implications for action
Four actions emerge from the analysis. First, a portfolio wide rate review is warranted, with particular attention to liability and property damage, which together carry two thirds of premium and have absorbed the bulk of the premium softening. Second, within liability, the bodily injury sub tariff needs recalibration against recent claims, because at nearly 5,000 EUR per claim it is the highest severity sub coverage and the line most exposed when overall pricing softens. Third, COMP_N either needs a higher loading or the reintroduction of an excess to align policyholder incentives with the rest of the book. Fourth, the bonus malus loading on the neutral and bad history tiers needs a refresh using 2024 claim data, because the gap between premium loading and observed loss has widened sharply on those tiers in particular.
The methodological takeaway is the value of separating profitability from volatility. A single loss ratio number hides which segments are losing money on average, which segments are stable but capital intensive, and which segments are both. Visual analytics makes those distinctions legible at a glance, and the heatmap, ridge plot, and bubble chart in this report each show a different facet of the same underlying portfolio.

The portfolio at a glance

Why is it rising? Pricing, not claims
The two loss making coverages
Bodily injury is the severity driver
Comprehensive without excess: structural issue
Pricing tiers that no longer work
The over 75 anomaly
Statistical validation of group differences
Volatility: a separate problem from profitability
Recommended actions
Reproducibility and references



This deck condenses a longer technical report into twelve slides. The first half (slides three through nine) tells the profitability story. The second half (slides ten and eleven) addresses volatility, which is a separate diagnostic from loss ratio and is what determines capital requirements. The methods are univariate distribution charts, bivariate group comparisons with statistical validation, and multivariate heatmaps and bubble charts. All work is in R using the tidyverse, ggplot2, and statistical extensions.

title: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
1. The portfolio at a glance










The book covers three calendar years from 2022 through 2024, with 352,000 policy years from 186,000 unique policyholders. Premium written totals 134 million EUR against 73 million EUR of incurred losses, giving an aggregate loss ratio of 70 percent. The product mix is dominated by CC (third party plus two additional covers) at 58 percent, with comprehensive variants (COMP_E and COMP_N) together accounting for 30 percent. The basic third party product is a small slice at 1.6 percent. This composition matters because the loss ratio story is concentrated in specific product segments that the rest of the deck will identify.

title: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
2. Loss ratio is rising










This is the headline. Loss ratio sat at 66 percent in 2022, held flat at 67 percent in 2023, then jumped to 75 percent in 2024. The 70 percent dashed line is the typical underwriting target for motor portfolios before expenses. The book crossed it in 2024. The relative move is 12 percent in two years, which is large for a stable line of business and demands attention. The rest of the deck explores why this happened and where the deterioration is concentrated, because a blanket rate increase would over charge segments that are not the source of the trouble.

title: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 3. Pricing, not claims, is the driver
text:
3. Pricing, not claims, is the driver








Frequency and severity flat. Premium per exposure fell 9 percent.


Metric
2022
2024
Change




Claim frequency
0.302
0.311
2.8%


Average premium
€446
€404
-9.3%


Severity per exposure
€293
€302
3.1%


Loss ratio
65.7%
74.7%
13.6%








Decomposing the loss ratio change between 2022 and 2024 isolates the cause, and the answer is not what intuition suggests. Claim frequency rose only 3 percent in two years, essentially flat. Severity per exposure rose around 3 percent, also essentially flat. Average premium per exposure, however, fell roughly 9 percent over the three years, from 446 euro to 404 euro. The arithmetic is unforgiving: loss ratio equals incurred divided by premium, and when the denominator shrinks while the numerator holds steady, loss ratio must rise. The deterioration is a pricing story, not a claims story. Rates have softened across every policy type, and the business mix shifted from heavily new business in 2022 toward renewals in 2024, both pointing to competitive pressure on rates rather than claims inflation.

title: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 4. Two coverages carry the load and run hot
text:
4. Two coverages carry the load and run hot










Decomposing premium by coverage line shows that two lines carry most of the volume and both are loss making. Liability is the largest line at 64 million EUR and runs a 74 percent loss ratio. Property damage is 26 million EUR and runs 72 percent. Together they account for 67 percent of premium. The four smallest lines (theft, fire, legal protection, occupants) all run between 40 and 52 percent and are clearly profitable. Glass is borderline at 65 percent. The conclusion is structural: the smaller profitable lines cannot cross subsidise the larger loss making ones at this scale. Any portfolio rebuild has to start with liability and property damage repricing.

title: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 5. Bodily injury is the severity driver
text:
5. Bodily injury is the severity driver










Within the liability line, claims split into property damage and bodily injury. The chart shows mean cost per claim for each sub coverage. Bodily injury averages 4,940 EUR per claim, more than four times the next largest category. Property damage averages 706 EUR. The 4,768 bodily injury claims represent about 9 percent of all claims but a disproportionate share of total claims cost. The pricing implication is direct: the bodily injury sub tariff has to track external inflation indicators, because medical and legal costs have been rising in Spain over the relevant period. This is the single most actionable finding in the deck.

title: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
6. Comprehensive without excess is structural










Comprehensive without excess (COMP_N) runs a 94 percent loss ratio overall and crossed 102 percent in 2024. The right panel shows why. Claim frequency on COMP_N is 1.05 per exposure, roughly five times the frequency on basic third party (TP) at 0.13. The mechanism is straightforward: removing the excess transfers all small claim cost from the policyholder to the insurer. Severity is comparable across products, ranging from 750 to 1,100 EUR. Frequency drives the result. The product can be made viable either by reintroducing an excess, which would align policyholder incentives, or by adding a substantial loading that fully prices the frequency effect. Pricing alone may not solve it given how aggressively frequency rises.

title: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
7. Bonus tiers no longer work










Bonus malus pricing should keep loss ratio roughly flat across tiers, because higher risk drivers pay a higher loading that offsets their elevated claims. The chart shows that is not what happened. Good history policies rose from 66 percent to 74 percent loss ratio over two years, a manageable move. Bad history policies rose from 53 percent to 93 percent, almost doubling. The neutral tier overshot to 101 percent in 2024. The pricing model loaded bad history drivers 2.2 times above good history drivers, but recent claim experience tells us the loading needs to be larger or the segments have deteriorated for reasons the tariff does not capture. A recalibration using 2024 data is overdue.

title: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
8. The over 75 anomaly










Loss ratio follows a U shape across driver age, which is standard in motor insurance. The 18-25 band runs 63 percent and the over 75 band jumps to 102 percent. The pricing line on top shows the loading. The 18-25 band is correctly priced at 887 EUR, well above portfolio average. From there premium falls steadily with age, and by the 76+ band it has dropped to 390 EUR, below the portfolio average. The over 75 segment is a small share of the book (about 0.8 percent) so the financial impact is limited, but it is a clear and identifiable pricing error. The senior tariff needs a review.

title: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
9. Group differences are statistically real










The visual differences in incurred amounts across policy types could in principle be due to chance. The Kruskal Wallis test, which is the nonparametric alternative to ANOVA, formally checks whether the distributions are the same. The chi square statistic is large and the p value is effectively zero, so the null hypothesis of equal distributions is rejected. The pairwise comparisons attached to the chart confirm that comprehensive products (COMP_E and COMP_N) concentrate around materially larger claim amounts than third party variants. The log scale on the y axis is essential, because the raw distribution would compress the structure into the bottom decile of the plot.

title: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
10. Volatility tells a different story










Loss ratio measures the average outcome. The coefficient of variation, defined as the standard deviation of incurred per policy divided by the mean, measures the dispersion that drives capital requirements. The heatmap inverts the loss ratio result. COMP_N, which has the worst loss ratio, has the lowest CV at around 2 to 4, because almost every policy claims and the distribution is dense. TPG and CC under good history cover have CV values between 14 and 18, because the typical policy never claims and the rare loss is very large. The two views are complementary. The CC book is profitable on average but capital intensive. COMP_N is loss making but the losses are predictable.

title: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
11. Recommended actions


Recalibrate the bodily injury sub tariff against 2024 claims data. At an average claim cost of nearly 5,000 euro, this is the highest severity sub coverage in the book and the line most exposed when overall pricing softens. Around 9 percent of claims drive a disproportionate share of cost.
Restructure COMP_N: either reintroduce an excess to align policyholder incentives with the rest of the book, or apply a substantial loading that prices the frequency effect properly. Pricing alone may not solve it.
Refresh the bonus malus loading on neutral and bad history tiers. The current 2.2x multiplier was sufficient in 2022 and is not in 2024. The neutral tier in particular crossed 100 percent loss ratio last year.
Review the senior tariff for drivers aged 76 and above. The segment is small but identifiably under loaded, with average premium below portfolio mean against a 102 percent loss ratio.
Reinsurance review for CC and TPG under good history cover, where the coefficient of variation is highest. These books are profitable on average but their tail risk is the capital constraint.



The five actions are ordered by impact rather than by ease of execution. The overall loss ratio rise is a premium softening story, so the immediate response is a portfolio wide rate review with particular attention to liability and property damage, which together carry two thirds of premium. Within liability, bodily injury repricing matters because it is the highest severity sub coverage in absolute terms and the line most exposed when overall pricing softens. COMP_N is a smaller book but the loss ratio is structural and will continue to deteriorate without intervention. The bonus malus refresh and senior tariff review are pricing model maintenance items that affect smaller segments. The reinsurance review addresses a different problem from the loss ratio fixes: it manages volatility on segments that are profitable but capital intensive. These problems demand separate responses, and treating them together would mean over correcting one of them.

title: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
12. Reproducibility and references



Methods
The full technical report documents every step from raw CSV to charts. Each figure is reproducible with set.seed(608) where sampling is used.
Univariate techniques: histograms, density plots, ordered bar charts.
Bivariate techniques: grouped bar charts, line trajectories, loss ratio heatmaps, scatter with bubble size.
Statistical validation: Kruskal Wallis with FDR adjusted pairwise comparisons, chi square for categorical relationships.
Volatility analysis: coefficient of variation by segment crossing.



Data and tools
Dataset: Mendeley Data, a real Spanish motor insurance portfolio across 2022 to 2024.
Framing: Swiss Re sigma 4/2019 on advanced analytics in P&C insurance.
R packages: tidyverse, lubridate, patchwork, ggdist, ggridges, ggstatsplot, ggcorrplot, scales, ggrepel, colorspace.
Reproducibility: full Quarto source and freeze: auto for stable rendering.




The accompanying technical report includes all data wrangling steps, including the recoding of policy types and bonus scores, the construction of derived variables such as licence tenure and value quintiles, and the helper function used to compute portfolio metrics consistently across charts. The Kruskal Wallis test was chosen over a parametric ANOVA because the incurred distribution is heavy tailed and the assumptions for ANOVA would be violated. All charts use perceptually uniform palettes from the colorspace package and the loss ratio heatmap is centred at the 70 percent profitability threshold so that the colour mid point carries analytical meaning. The deck is reproducible from the same CSV with the Quarto source in the project repository.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
Show the code
pacman::p_load(
  tidyverse, lubridate, patchwork,
  ggdist, ggridges, ggstatsplot, corrplot,
  scales, ggrepel, knitr, colorspace
)

raw <- read_delim("data/Motor_insurance.csv", delim = ";", show_col_types = FALSE)

ins <- raw |>
  drop_na(fuel_type, vehicle_value, vehicle_age, age_driving_licence) |>
  mutate(
    policy_type = factor(policy_type,
      levels = c("TP", "TPG", "CC", "COMP_E", "COMP_N"),
      labels = c("TP", "TPG", "CC", "COMP_E", "COMP_N")),
    bonus_score = factor(bonus_score,
      levels = c("G", "N", "B"),
      labels = c("Good", "Neutral", "Bad")),
    year = factor(year),
    age_band = cut(driver_age,
      breaks = c(17, 25, 35, 45, 55, 65, 75, 99),
      labels = c("18-25", "26-35", "36-45", "46-55", "56-65", "66-75", "76+"),
      ordered_result = TRUE)
  )

portfolio_metrics <- function(df, ...) {
  df |>
    group_by(...) |>
    summarise(
      policies = n(),
      exposure = sum(total_exposure, na.rm = TRUE),
      premium = sum(total_premium, na.rm = TRUE),
      incurred = sum(total_incurred, na.rm = TRUE),
      claims = sum(total_claims, na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) |>
    mutate(
      loss_ratio = incurred / premium,
      claim_freq = claims / exposure,
      avg_premium = premium / exposure,
      severity = incurred / pmax(claims, 1)
    )
}

acc <- "#0072B2"
warm <- "#D55E00"
green <- "#009E73"
neutral <- "#999999"

theme_slide <- function() {
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 13),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
    )
}

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
2 1. The portfolio at a glance


Show the code
snapshot <- tibble(
  metric = c("Policy-years", "Unique policyholders", "Premium written", "Total incurred",
             "Average premium", "Claim frequency", "Loss ratio"),
  value = c(scales::comma(nrow(ins)),
            scales::comma(length(unique(ins$insured_id))),
            paste0("\u20ac", scales::comma(round(sum(ins$total_premium)/1e6, 1)), "M"),
            paste0("\u20ac", scales::comma(round(sum(ins$total_incurred)/1e6, 1)), "M"),
            paste0("\u20ac", round(sum(ins$total_premium)/sum(ins$total_exposure))),
            scales::number(sum(ins$total_claims)/sum(ins$total_exposure), accuracy = 0.001),
            scales::percent(sum(ins$total_incurred)/sum(ins$total_premium), accuracy = 0.1))
)

p1 <- ggplot(snapshot, aes(x = 1, y = fct_rev(factor(metric, levels = metric)))) +
  geom_text(aes(label = value), hjust = 0, size = 5.5, fontface = "bold", colour = acc) +
  geom_text(aes(label = metric, x = -0.05), hjust = 1, size = 4.5, colour = "grey30") +
  xlim(-2, 4) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA))

mix <- ins |>
  count(policy_type) |>
  mutate(pct = n/sum(n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_reorder(policy_type, n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = acc, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)), hjust = -0.1, size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2)), labels = comma) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Product mix", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_slide()

p1 + mix + plot_layout(widths = c(1, 1))










The book covers three calendar years from 2022 through 2024, with 352,000 policy years from 186,000 unique policyholders. Premium written totals 134 million EUR against 73 million EUR of incurred losses, giving an aggregate loss ratio of 70 percent. The product mix is dominated by CC (third party plus two additional covers) at 58 percent, with comprehensive variants (COMP_E and COMP_N) together accounting for 30 percent. The basic third party product is a small slice at 1.6 percent. This composition matters because the loss ratio story is concentrated in specific product segments that the rest of the deck will identify.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery

3 2. Loss ratio is rising


Show the code
yearly <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(year)

ggplot(yearly, aes(x = year, y = loss_ratio, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(colour = warm, linewidth = 1.5) +

    title = "Portfolio loss ratio: 67% \u2192 75% in two years",
    subtitle = "12 percent relative deterioration. The 2024 jump is the immediate concern.",
    x = NULL, y = "Loss ratio"
  ) +
  theme_slide()

The rest of the deck explores why this happened and where the deterioration is concentrated, because a blanket rate increase would over charge segments that are not the source of the trouble.

section: 4 3. Pricing, not claims, is the driver
text:
4 3. Pricing, not claims, is the driver


Show the code
yearly_decomp <- yearly |>
  mutate(severity_per_exp = incurred / exposure)

y2022 <- yearly_decomp |> filter(year == "2022")
y2024 <- yearly_decomp |> filter(year == "2024")

decomp_tbl <- tibble(
  Metric = c("Claim frequency", "Average premium", "Severity per exposure", "Loss ratio"),
  `2022` = c(number(y2022$claim_freq, accuracy = 0.001),
              dollar(y2022$avg_premium, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1),
              dollar(y2022$severity_per_exp, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1),
              percent(y2022$loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1)),
  `2024` = c(number(y2024$claim_freq, accuracy = 0.001),
              dollar(y2024$avg_premium, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1),
              dollar(y2024$severity_per_exp, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1),
              percent(y2024$loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1)),
  Change = c(percent(y2024$claim_freq/y2022$claim_freq - 1, accuracy = 0.1),
             percent(y2024$avg_premium/y2022$avg_premium - 1, accuracy = 0.1),
             percent(y2024$severity_per_exp/y2022$severity_per_exp - 1, accuracy = 0.1),
             percent(y2024$loss_ratio/y2022$loss_ratio - 1, accuracy = 0.1))
)
