Show the code
make_density <- function(var, title, fill_col) {
  ggplot(ins, aes(x = .data[[var]])) +
    geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                   bins = 30, fill = fill_col, alpha = 0.85, colour = "white") +
    geom_density(colour = "grey20", linewidth = 0.5) +
    labs(title = title, x = "Years", y = NULL) +
    theme_va() +
    theme(axis.text.y = element_blank())
}

p_driver <- make_density("driver_age", "Driver age", acc)
p_vehicle <- make_density("vehicle_age", "Vehicle age", warm)
p_licence <- make_density("licence_tenure", "Licence tenure", "#009E73")

p_driver + p_vehicle + p_licence





Distributions of driver age, vehicle age, and licence tenure. The shared scale on the x axis is intentional because all three are measured in years.










TipReading the plot



Driver age is roughly symmetric with a median of 49 years and an interquartile range from 39 to 58, consistent with the chart’s peak in the late forties. Vehicle age has a median of 16 years and a heavier right tail, with the third quartile at 23 and the maximum above 80, which puts a substantial population of older vehicles in the book. Licence tenure has a median of 24 years and is the most unimodal of the three, with a peak around twenty years and a thin left tail of newly licensed drivers. The two age variables span wide enough ranges that age band and value quintile transformations will be used in the bivariate analysis to control for these heavy tails.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
5.3 Categorical variables
The portfolio descriptors get a faceted bar chart so they can be read together. Bars are ordered by inherent meaning where one exists (coverage breadth, bonus score) and by frequency otherwise.


Show the code
make_bar <- function(var, title) {
  ins |>
    count(.data[[var]]) |>
    mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = .data[[var]], y = n)) +
    geom_col(fill = acc, alpha = 0.85) +
    geom_text(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)),
              vjust = -0.4, size = 3, colour = "grey20") +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)), labels = comma) +
    labs(title = title, x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_va() +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8))
}

p1 <- make_bar("year", "Policy year")
p2 <- make_bar("policy_type", "Policy type") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7))
p3 <- make_bar("policy_status", "Policy status")
p4 <- make_bar("business_type", "Business origin")
p5 <- make_bar("payment_frequency", "Payment frequency")
p6 <- make_bar("bonus_score", "Bonus score")
p7 <- make_bar("fuel_type", "Fuel type")
p8 <- make_bar("circulation_area", "Circulation area")

(p1 + p2 + p3) /
(p4 + p5 + p6) /
(p7 + p8 + plot_spacer())





Composition of the portfolio across eight categorical descriptors. Percentages are calculated within each panel.










TipReading the plot



The book is growing rapidly. 2024 contributes 47 percent of all policy years, up from 19 percent in 2022, so almost half of everything in this dataset is recent. CC (third party plus two additional covers) is the dominant product at 58 percent, followed by comprehensive with excess at 26 percent. Comprehensive without excess (COMP_N) and basic third party (TP) are the smallest segments. The portfolio is heavily skewed toward annual paying customers with a good bonus history, on diesel vehicles, in inland municipalities. The 11 percent cancellation share is a downstream signal worth tracking, because cancelled policies often correlate with claims related friction.








NoteNotes on the design



A small detail in the bar chart matters: the y axis scale uses expand to push the upper bound 15 percent above the tallest bar so the percentage labels do not collide with the panel border. This is a tiny adjustment but it is the difference between a chart that looks rushed and one that looks intentional. Direct labels also remove the need for a y axis altogether in principle, which honours the data ink ratio (the share of plot ink that conveys data rather than decoration). The axis is kept here only because the panel shares its style with bars used later that lack direct labels.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
5.4 Vehicle brand: long tail categorical
Vehicle brand has 68 distinct values, which exceeds what a bar chart can show legibly. The top 15 brands are plotted and the rest are grouped as “other”, then sort by frequency.


Show the code
brand_tbl <- ins |>
  count(vehicle_brand, sort = TRUE) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))

top15 <- head(brand_tbl, 15)
other_total <- sum(brand_tbl$n[-(1:15)])
other_pct <- other_total / sum(brand_tbl$n)

top15_plot <- bind_rows(
  top15,
  tibble(vehicle_brand = "Other (53 brands)", n = other_total, pct = other_pct)
) |>
  mutate(vehicle_brand = fct_reorder(vehicle_brand, n))

ggplot(top15_plot, aes(x = n, y = vehicle_brand)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = vehicle_brand == "Other (53 brands)"), alpha = 0.85, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(comma(n), " (", percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1), ")")),
            hjust = -0.05, size = 3.2, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = acc, "TRUE" = neutral)) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18)), labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "The brand mix is concentrated in volume European marques",
    subtitle = "Top 15 brands account for 92 percent of all policy years",
    x = "Number of policy years",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())





Top 15 vehicle brands by policy year count. The remaining 53 brands together account for 8 percent of the portfolio.










TipReading the plot



The seven largest brands are all European models, with Renault, Citroen, and Peugeot leading. Premium German marques such as Audi, Mercedes, and BMW appear next, which is relevant because these brands carry higher vehicle values and higher repair costs. The shape of this distribution is long tail. A handful of brands dominate, and the remaining 53 brands sit in a thin tail that contributes 8 percent of the book.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
6.1 Year on year deterioration
The first bivariate question is the temporal one. Has the portfolio held its profitability over the three years, or is something moving?


Show the code
yearly <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(year)

p_lr <- ggplot(yearly, aes(x = year, y = loss_ratio)) +
  geom_col(fill = warm, alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, colour = "grey50", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Loss ratio", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_va()

p_freq <- ggplot(yearly, aes(x = year, y = claim_freq)) +
  geom_col(fill = acc, alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = number(claim_freq, accuracy = 0.001)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Claim frequency", subtitle = "Claims per unit of exposure", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_va()

p_prem <- ggplot(yearly, aes(x = year, y = avg_premium)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#009E73", alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollar(avg_premium, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Average premium per exposure", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_va()

p_lr + p_freq + p_prem





Aggregate portfolio metrics by policy year. Both loss ratio and average premium have risen in 2024.










TipReading the plot



Loss ratio rose from 66 percent in 2022 to 75 percent in 2024, a 14 percent relative increase. Claim frequency drifted up only slightly, from 0.303 to 0.312, which is essentially flat. Average premium per exposure moved in the wrong direction, falling from 446 EUR to 404 EUR over the three years. The arithmetic follows directly: pricing is softening while claim cost holds steady, and the loss ratio is the result. This chart sets the agenda for everything that follows. The question is no longer whether the book is deteriorating, but which segments are pulling the loss ratio up.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
6.2 Loss ratio by policy type
Policy type is the most informative segmentation in motor insurance because it determines the coverage breadth and therefore the universe of claims the insurer pays.


Show the code
by_type <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(policy_type)

ggplot(by_type, aes(x = policy_type, y = loss_ratio)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = loss_ratio > 0.7), alpha = 0.85, width = 0.65, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.7, colour = "grey50", linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.6, y = 0.72, label = "70 percent target",
           colour = "grey40", size = 3, hjust = 0) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#009E73", "TRUE" = warm)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)),
                     limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Comprehensive without excess is the loss-ratio outlier",
    subtitle = "Loss ratio aggregated as total incurred over total premium across all three years",
    x = NULL, y = "Loss ratio"
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8))





Loss ratio by policy type, with average premium overlaid. COMP_N runs a markedly higher loss ratio despite charging the highest premium.










TipReading the plot



The five policy types fall into three bands. TP (third party only) and CC (third party plus two additional covers) sit in the green zone, comfortably under the 70 percent reference line. TPG (third party plus glass) sits just under at 69 percent. COMP_E (comprehensive with excess) crosses the line at 74 percent. COMP_N (comprehensive without excess) sits in its own band at 94 percent, very close to the underwriting break even point before expenses are even loaded. The pattern is consistent with insurance theory: removing the excess transfers all small claim cost from the policyholder to the insurer, so claim frequency should rise sharply. The next chart confirms whether that is what is happening.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 6.3 Decomposing the loss ratio into frequency and severity
text:
6.3 Decomposing the loss ratio into frequency and severity
Loss ratio equals claim frequency multiplied by claim severity divided by average premium. Plotting frequency and severity side by side reveals which lever is pulling.


Show the code
by_type_long <- by_type |>
  select(policy_type, claim_freq, severity) |>
  pivot_longer(c(claim_freq, severity), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  mutate(metric = recode(metric,
                         claim_freq = "Claim frequency (claims per exposure)",
                         severity = "Severity (mean incurred per claim)"))

ggplot(by_type_long, aes(x = policy_type, y = value, fill = metric)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(metric == "Claim frequency (claims per exposure)",
                               number(value, accuracy = 0.01),
                               dollar(value, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1))),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(acc, warm)) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  facet_wrap(~ metric, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL,
       title = "Frequency, not severity, drives the COMP_N problem") +
  theme_va() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7),
        strip.text = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"))





Frequency-severity decomposition by policy type. COMP_N has 5x the frequency of TP, while severity is comparable across types.










TipReading the plot



Severity is stable across policy types, sitting between 750 EUR and 1,100 EUR per claim. The cost of an individual claim does not change much with the product. Frequency, however, varies by a factor of nearly eight, from 0.13 claims per exposure on TP to 1.05 on COMP_N. COMP_N policyholders are not having worse accidents. They are claiming for every small loss, because there is no excess to absorb small losses. The pricing implication is direct: COMP_N should either carry a much higher loading or reintroduce an excess to align incentives with the rest of the book.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 6.4 How the deterioration is concentrated by policy type and year
text:
6.4 How the deterioration is concentrated by policy type and year
The previous two charts looked at aggregates over three years. The next chart asks whether the loss ratio uplift in 2024 is broad or concentrated. Hovering on a line highlights that policy type and fades the others, which makes individual trajectories easier to compare.


Show the code
by_type_year <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(policy_type, year)

by_type_year <- by_type_year |>
  mutate(
    tooltip = paste0(
      "<b>", policy_type, "</b><br>",
      "Year: ", year, "<br>",
      "Loss ratio: ", percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1), "<br>",
      "Claim frequency: ", number(claim_freq, accuracy = 0.001), "<br>",
      "Avg premium: ", dollar(avg_premium, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1)
    )
  )

p_trajectories <- ggplot(by_type_year,
                          aes(x = year, y = loss_ratio,
                              group = policy_type, colour = policy_type)) +
  geom_line_interactive(aes(data_id = policy_type, tooltip = policy_type),
                        linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point_interactive(aes(data_id = policy_type, tooltip = tooltip),
                         size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, colour = "grey60", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Loss ratio trajectories diverge after 2022",
    subtitle = "COMP_N crossed 100 percent in 2024",
    x = NULL, y = "Loss ratio", colour = "Policy type"
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7)
  )

girafe(
  ggobj = p_trajectories,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 5,
  options = list(
    opts_hover(css = "stroke-width:2.5px;opacity:1;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
  )
)




Loss ratio by policy type and year. Hover any line to isolate that policy type’s trajectory.








TipReading the plot



Every policy type except CC is moving up. COMP_N rose from 73 percent to 102 percent in two years, and is the only line that crosses the underwriting break even point. COMP_E and TPG climbed in step from 60 to 75 percent. TP (the smallest book) more than doubled from 30 percent to 65 percent. CC, the largest book, oscillated around 60 percent and is the only segment holding steady. The portfolio has three distinct problem children with different trajectories. A blanket rate increase would over charge CC customers who are not the source of the trouble.








NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



geom_line_interactive and geom_point_interactive are the ggiraph counterparts of the standard ggplot geoms. The data_id aesthetic groups the line and the three points for one policy type so they highlight together on hover, and the opts_hover_inv option fades unhovered elements to 20 percent opacity. This is the linked highlighting pattern from the hands on exercise on interactive graphics, applied to a line chart instead of a scatter.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 6.5 Bonus score and the pricing versus experience gap
text:
6.5 Bonus score and the pricing versus experience gap
Bonus score is a proxy for the insurer’s prior view of the policyholder, set at underwriting. A policyholder with a bad claims history pays a higher premium, but the question is whether the loading is sufficient.


Show the code
by_bonus <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(bonus_score)

p_prem <- ggplot(by_bonus, aes(x = bonus_score, y = avg_premium)) +
  geom_col(fill = acc, alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollar(avg_premium, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Average premium per exposure", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_va()

p_lr <- ggplot(by_bonus, aes(x = bonus_score, y = loss_ratio)) +
  geom_col(fill = warm, alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.7, colour = "grey50", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Loss ratio", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_va()

p_prem + p_lr





Average premium and loss ratio by bonus score. Bad-history policyholders pay 2.2x more in premium yet still produce the highest loss ratio.










TipReading the plot



Pricing tiers in a textbook bonus malus system should produce the opposite of what this chart shows. Good history drivers should run a low loss ratio at low premium, neutral drivers should sit roughly at break even, and bad history drivers should run a low loss ratio at high premium because the loading more than offsets their elevated risk. What the data shows instead is a U shape on loss ratio, with good drivers at 71 percent and bad drivers at 85 percent. The bad history loading of 2.2x is not pulling its weight. Either the bad history segment is being under priced or the loading is correctly calibrated and the segment has worsened over time. A look at the year on year breakdown helps choose between the two explanations.




Show the code
by_bonus_year <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(bonus_score, year)

ggplot(by_bonus_year, aes(x = year, y = loss_ratio,
                          group = bonus_score, colour = bonus_score)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, colour = "grey60", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Good history" = "#009E73",
                                  "Neutral" = neutral,
                                  "Bad history" = warm)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Bad-history policies deteriorated sharply",
    subtitle = "53 percent loss ratio in 2022 became 93 percent in 2024",
    x = NULL, y = "Loss ratio", colour = "Bonus score"
  ) +
  theme_va()





Loss ratio by bonus score and year. The bad-history loss ratio almost doubled from 2022 to 2024.










TipReading the plot



Bad history policies climbed from 53 percent to 93 percent loss ratio in two years, and the neutral segment overshot bad history briefly in 2024 at 101 percent. Good history policies climbed too but stayed inside acceptable territory. Against a backdrop of softening premium across the whole book, the loadings for the risky segments have not kept pace with experience. The pricing model needs the bad and neutral tiers recalibrated using recent claim data rather than a uniform rate increase across all tiers.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 6.6 Driver age and the loss ratio inversion
text:
6.6 Driver age and the loss ratio inversion
Driver age in motor insurance follows a well established U shape: youngest and oldest drivers have the highest risk, middle aged drivers the lowest. The question is whether this book follows the textbook pattern, and whether pricing reflects it.


Show the code
by_age <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(age_band)

# Two y axes are reasonable here because both are continuous and meaningfully connected to the x axis
sc <- max(by_age$avg_premium) / max(by_age$loss_ratio)

ggplot(by_age, aes(x = age_band)) +
  geom_col(aes(y = loss_ratio), fill = warm, alpha = 0.75, width = 0.7) +
  geom_line(aes(y = avg_premium / sc, group = 1), colour = acc, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(y = avg_premium / sc), colour = acc, size = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(y = loss_ratio, label = percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.2, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_text(aes(y = avg_premium / sc,
                label = dollar(avg_premium, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -1.0, size = 3.2, colour = acc) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.7, colour = "grey50", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Loss ratio",
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . * sc,
                        name = "Average premium per exposure",
                        labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Pricing falls as drivers age, but loss ratio spikes again at 76+",
    subtitle = "The 18-25 band is correctly loaded. The 76+ band is not.",
    x = NULL
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(
    axis.title.y.left = element_text(colour = warm),
    axis.title.y.right = element_text(colour = acc)
  )





Loss ratio and average premium by driver age band. The 76+ band shows a sharp loss ratio spike that pricing does not anticipate.










TipReading the plot



The U shape on loss ratio is visible, with 18-25 at 63 percent, the middle bands hovering between 68 and 73 percent, and 76+ jumping to 102 percent. The average premium curve does part of the right thing. It loads the 18-25 band correctly at 887 EUR, well above the portfolio average. However, the loading flattens after that, and at the over 75 end pricing drops back to 390 EUR, below the average. The pricing model treats older drivers as low risk when the data says they have crossed the underwriting break even point. The 76+ segment is small (about 0.8 percent of the book), so the financial impact is limited, but it is a clear signal that the senior driver tariff needs review.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
6.7 Statistical validation with Kruskal Wallis
The visual differences across groups raise an obvious question. Could the differences be due to chance? The distribution of incurred amounts per policy is heavy tailed and far from normal, so a parametric ANOVA would mislead. The Kruskal Wallis test is the nonparametric equivalent and is robust to non normality. The ggstatsplot package puts the statistical annotation directly on the plot, which means the test result and the visual evidence sit together for the reader.


Show the code
# Work with claims-only subset to avoid the log(0) trap, and downsample for ggstatsplot performance
set.seed(608)
claims_sample <- ins |>
  filter(total_incurred > 0) |>
  slice_sample(n = 5000)

ggbetweenstats(
  data = claims_sample,
  x = policy_type,
  y = total_incurred,
  type = "nonparametric",
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  centrality.point.args = list(size = 4, color = warm),
  violin.args = list(width = 0.7, alpha = 0.4),
  title = "Incurred per claiming policy differs significantly across policy types",
  xlab = NULL,
  ylab = "Total incurred (log scale)"
) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac")) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7))





Distribution of log incurred (for policies with claims) by policy type, with Kruskal-Wallis test annotation. The test confirms that the differences in incurred amount across policy types are statistically significant.










TipReading the plot



The Kruskal Wallis chi squared statistic is large and the p value is effectively zero, confirming that incurred amounts are not drawn from the same distribution across policy types. The pairwise comparisons (only significant ones shown) attach numerical evidence to the visual differences in the violins. COMP_E and COMP_N concentrate around larger claim amounts than the third party variants, with median incurred about double. The log scale on the y axis is essential here because the raw incurred distribution would crush all the structure into the bottom decile of the plot.








NoteNotes on the design



ggstatsplot is doing two jobs in one chart: it draws the violin and the boxplot in a single composite, and it runs the Kruskal Wallis test plus pairwise post hoc comparisons and writes the results into the subtitle. The principle in play is that the test belongs on the plot. A reader who sees a difference between groups will ask “is it significant?” within a second of seeing the visual, and the conventional answer (a separate table on the next page) breaks the reader’s flow. Embedding the statistic where the question forms is the move.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 6.9 Statistical validation: claim frequency by payment frequency
text:
6.9 Statistical validation: claim frequency by payment frequency
The previous test compared continuous incurred amounts. Claim frequency is a count rate, and the question of whether it differs by payment frequency is a categorical on count problem. A chi square test on a 2x2 contingency of “claim or not” by payment frequency answers it.


Show the code
ins_test <- ins |>
  mutate(any_claim = total_claims > 0)

ggbarstats(
  data = ins_test,
  x = any_claim,
  y = payment_frequency,
  type = "parametric",
  bf.message = FALSE,
  proportion.test = TRUE,
  label = "percentage",
  title = "Claim probability is higher for quarterly payers",
  xlab = NULL,
  ylab = "Share of policies",
  legend.title = "At least one claim"
) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = warm, "FALSE" = "#a6cee3")) +
  theme_va()





Share of policies with at least one claim, by payment frequency. The chi-square test confirms quarterly payers claim significantly more often.










TipReading the plot



The chi square statistic is highly significant. Quarterly payers are about 30 percent more likely to file at least one claim than annual payers. This is a standard underwriting signal: payment frequency is correlated with cash flow stress, and policyholders under cash flow stress claim differently. Pricing the quarterly tier higher is already standard practice in most insurers, and this chart shows the loading is justified, though whether it is large enough is a separate question that the loss ratio chart for payment frequency would answer.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 7.1 Heatmap: loss ratio by age band and policy type
text:
7.1 Heatmap: loss ratio by age band and policy type


Show the code
hm <- ins |>
  portfolio_metrics(age_band, policy_type) |>
  mutate(reliable = policies >= 200)

ggplot(hm, aes(x = policy_type, y = age_band, fill = loss_ratio)) +
  geom_tile(aes(alpha = reliable), colour = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(reliable, percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 1), "")),
            size = 3, colour = "grey15") +
  scale_fill_continuous_diverging(palette = "Blue-Red 3", mid = 0.7,
                                    name = "Loss ratio", labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_alpha_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = 1, `FALSE` = 0.2), guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Loss ratio runs hottest for middle-aged drivers on comprehensive cover",
    subtitle = "Diverging palette centred at the 70 percent profitability threshold",
    x = NULL, y = "Driver age band"
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7),
    legend.position = "right"
  )





Loss ratio heatmap with cells annotated. White cells indicate fewer than 200 policies, where the ratio is too noisy to read.










TipReading the plot



The hottest cells are the comprehensive products at every middle age band, with several crossing 90 percent. The 76+ row is consistently red across products, with CC reaching 128 percent. The 18-25 row, in contrast, is mostly white or blue, suggesting that despite the high per policy premium the segment is correctly underwritten. The colour scale is diverging at 70 percent rather than at zero, because the meaningful break for a P&C insurer is the loss ratio threshold above which the line stops being profitable, not the mathematical zero. Centering the divergence at the analytically meaningful value is a small choice that completely changes how the chart reads.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 7.2 Ridge plot: incurred distribution by year and bonus score
text:
7.2 Ridge plot: incurred distribution by year and bonus score
The previous heatmap showed central tendencies. The next chart shows whole distributions, which is where the tail behaviour and volatility surface.


Show the code
ridge_data <- ins |>
  filter(total_incurred > 0)

ggplot(ridge_data, aes(x = total_incurred, y = bonus_score, fill = bonus_score)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.7, scale = 0.95, quantile_lines = TRUE,
                      quantiles = c(0.5)) +
  facet_wrap(~ year, ncol = 3) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Good history" = "#009E73",
                                "Neutral" = neutral,
                                "Bad history" = warm)) +
  labs(
    title = "Incurred amounts grow heavier-tailed for bad-history policies",
    subtitle = "Vertical line marks the median",
    x = "Total incurred (log scale)", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10))





Distribution of log incurred for claiming policies, by bonus score and year. The right tail is heavy across all groups but most pronounced for bad-history policies.










TipReading the plot



Three observations register. First, the median (the vertical line inside each ridge) drifts slightly to the right over time, but the movement is modest, consistent with the small severity per exposure rise observed in the year on year chart. Second, bad history policies sit at a higher median across all three years, as expected. Third, the right tails (anything above 5,000 EUR on the log axis) are heaviest for bad history policies in 2024, with a small visible bulge above 50,000 EUR that is absent in 2022. Volatility is concentrated in the bad history right tail. This matters for capital allocation: the rare big claim is what consumes reserves, and the bad history book has more of them.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 7.3 Faceted scatter: severity vs frequency by policy type
text:
7.3 Faceted scatter: severity vs frequency by policy type
A bubble chart with frequency on the x axis, severity on the y axis, and bubble size proportional to premium volume is the canonical actuarial diagnostic for understanding which segments earn what they need to earn.


Show the code
fs <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(policy_type, year)

ggplot(fs, aes(x = claim_freq, y = severity, size = premium, colour = policy_type)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = paste0(policy_type, " ", year)),
                  size = 2.6, max.overlaps = 20, segment.alpha = 0.3) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 14), labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"),
                         name = "Premium volume") +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1", guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Claim frequency (claims per exposure)") +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Severity (incurred per claim)",
                     labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac")) +
  labs(
    title = "COMP_N occupies its own quadrant: high frequency, low severity",
    subtitle = "Bubble area is proportional to premium volume"
  ) +
  theme_va()





Severity-frequency-volume map by policy type and year. Distance from the origin reflects expected loss cost per exposure.










TipReading the plot



COMP_N forms a tight cluster on the far right at high frequency and low severity, the pattern that follows from a deductible free product where every small bump is claimable. TP sits on the bottom left at low frequency and low severity, as expected given its narrow coverage. CC, the largest premium volume on the chart, sits in the lower middle and is the workhorse. COMP_E moves up and to the right between 2022 and 2024, a worrying trajectory because both frequency and severity are rising for the comprehensive with excess product. Distance from the origin equals expected loss cost per exposure, so a point in the upper right is structurally more expensive to insure regardless of how the loss ratio currently reads.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 7.4 Parallel coordinates: comparing segments across many measures
text:
7.4 Parallel coordinates: comparing segments across many measures
A parallel coordinates plot is the canonical multivariate view when many continuous measures need to be compared across a small number of groups. Each variable becomes a vertical axis, each group becomes a polyline that crosses the axes, and the shape of the polyline encodes the segment’s joint profile.


Show the code
pcp_data <- ins |>
  group_by(policy_type) |>
  summarise(
    `Loss ratio` = sum(total_incurred) / sum(total_premium),
    `Claim frequency` = sum(total_claims) / sum(total_exposure),
    `Avg premium` = sum(total_premium) / sum(total_exposure),
    `Avg severity` = sum(total_incurred) / pmax(sum(total_claims), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggparcoord(pcp_data,
           columns = 2:5,
           groupColumn = 1,
           scale = "uniminmax",
           showPoints = TRUE,
           alphaLines = 0.8,
           splineFactor = TRUE) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Policy type profiles across four portfolio metrics",
       subtitle = "Axes rescaled to [0,1] so the shape of the polyline conveys the joint profile",
       x = NULL, y = "Normalised value",
       colour = "Policy type") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))





Parallel coordinates plot of portfolio metrics by policy type. Each polyline is one policy type. The shape reveals which product profile differs the most across the four measures.










TipReading the plot



The polylines reveal each product’s structural profile. COMP_N climbs to the top of the claim frequency axis and the loss ratio axis while sitting near the middle on average premium and at the bottom on severity. The shape is consistent with a product priced as if it covers occasional accidents that pays out on every small bump, because there is no excess. TP runs along the bottom of frequency and loss ratio with the lowest premium, which is the expected profile for a stripped down third party product. CC and COMP_E sit in between and trace similar shapes, which is consistent with their being variants of the same comprehensive concept. The parallel coordinates view makes that family resemblance visible in a way that the separate bivariate charts do not.








NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



The ggparcoord function comes from the GGally package and accepts column indices rather than aesthetic mappings, which is unusual for ggplot2 extensions but matches the convention in the hands on materials. The scale = "uniminmax" argument rescales each axis to the 0 to 1 range so the chart compares shape rather than magnitude, and without it the average premium axis would dominate because it is on a hundred times larger scale. The splineFactor = TRUE argument smooths the polyline between axes, which is a small cosmetic gain that helps when only a handful of groups are plotted.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
7.5 Interactive heatmap with hierarchical clustering
The static loss ratio heatmap above shows the structure but does not let a reader interact with cells or read precise values. A second view through heatmaply produces an interactive heatmap with row and column dendrograms, on demand tooltips and the option to normalise the data and reorder by clustering. The matrix here is exposure weighted average loss ratio by driver age band and policy type, and to demonstrate normalisation, the data is z scored across each policy type column.


Show the code
heat_matrix <- ins |>
  group_by(age_band, policy_type) |>
  summarise(loss_ratio = sum(total_incurred) / sum(total_premium),
            policies = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(policies >= 200) |>
  select(-policies) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = policy_type, values_from = loss_ratio) |>
  column_to_rownames("age_band") |>
  as.matrix()

heatmaply(heat_matrix,
          scale = "column",
          dist_method = "euclidean",
          hclust_method = "ward.D",
          colors = diverging_hcl(50, palette = "Blue-Red 3"),
          k_row = 3,
          k_col = 2,
          margins = c(60, 80, 40, 20),
          fontsize_row = 9,
          fontsize_col = 9,
          main = "Loss ratio by age band and policy type, z scored per column",
          xlab = "Policy type",
          ylab = "Driver age band")




Interactive heatmap of loss ratio by age band and policy type, with hierarchical clustering on both axes. Each column is z scored, so the colour shows where each policy type runs hotter or cooler than its own portfolio average.








TipReading the plot



The dendrograms group age bands and policy types by how they behave across the rest of the matrix. The 76+ row sits at the top, separated from the rest, which confirms that the senior driver pattern is not a quirk of one policy type but a structural anomaly that crosses every product. On the column dendrogram, COMP_N pairs with COMP_E because both are comprehensive variants, and TPG sits alone, because it is the only stripped down product with both third party and additional cover. The colour reads relative to each column’s own mean, so the dark red cells point to age × product combinations that are unusually loss making for that product, not in absolute terms. This is a different question from the static heatmap above, which read in absolute loss ratio terms.








NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



The scale = "column" argument z scores each column independently, which is necessary here because COMP_N runs structurally hotter than TP across every age band. Without column scaling, the heatmap would collapse to a single column gradient and the row dendrogram would carry no information. The choice of Ward linkage and Euclidean distance follows the recipe in the multivariate analysis materials, and the k_row and k_col arguments draw rectangles around the top level clusters in each dimension. The diverging_hcl palette from colorspace gives a perceptually balanced blue to red diverging scale, which is the correct family for z scores because they cross zero.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 7.6 Interactive scatter: severity, frequency and volume with tooltip drill down
text:
7.6 Interactive scatter: severity, frequency and volume with tooltip drill down
The severity versus frequency bubble chart presented earlier as a faceted static panel is more useful when the reader can hover over a bubble to read the year and the exact metric values. The ggiraph package adds interactive geometries to ggplot2 without changing the grammar, so the same chart can be rebuilt as a hover and click target.


Show the code
fs <- ins |> portfolio_metrics(policy_type, year)

fs_tooltip <- fs |>
  mutate(
    tip = paste0(
      "<b>", policy_type, ", ", year, "</b><br>",
      "Loss ratio: ", scales::percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1), "<br>",
      "Claim frequency: ", scales::number(claim_freq, accuracy = 0.01), "<br>",
      "Severity: ", scales::dollar(severity, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1), "<br>",
      "Premium: ", scales::dollar(premium / 1e6, prefix = "\u20ac", suffix = "M", accuracy = 0.1)
    )
  )

p_interactive <- ggplot(fs_tooltip,
                       aes(x = claim_freq, y = severity,
                           size = premium, colour = policy_type)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = tip, data_id = policy_type),
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_text(aes(label = year), size = 2.8, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_size(range = c(4, 18), labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac", scale = 1e-6, suffix = "M")) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac")) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(
    title = "Severity, frequency and volume by policy type and year",
    subtitle = "Hover for details. Hover on a policy type to highlight its three years.",
    x = "Claim frequency", y = "Severity per claim",
    size = "Premium volume", colour = "Policy type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

girafe(
  ggobj = p_interactive,
  width_svg = 9,
  height_svg = 5,
  options = list(
    opts_hover(css = "stroke-width:2px;opacity:1;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
  )
)




Interactive bubble chart. Hovering on a bubble reveals the policy type, year, loss ratio, frequency, severity, and premium volume. Hovering on a policy type highlights all three of its bubbles across years.








TipReading the plot



The interactive version answers a question the static one cannot, which is what happens to a specific product across the three years. Hovering on COMP_E highlights its three bubbles and the polyline they implicitly trace, drifting up and to the right between 2022 and 2024. The hover effect on the rest of the chart fades to 20 percent opacity, which is the same Cairo haze technique used in the hands on materials. The chart serves both an overview function and a details on demand function, applying the standard interaction pattern of overview first, zoom and filter, details on demand.








NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



The geom_point_interactive geom from ggiraph accepts standard ggplot2 aesthetics plus two extras, tooltip and data_id. The tooltip aesthetic determines the content displayed on hover and accepts HTML. The data_id aesthetic groups elements that should highlight together, which is what produces the linked highlighting across years for one policy type. The girafe call at the end renders the ggplot to an interactive SVG and accepts CSS based hover styling through the opts_hover and opts_hover_inv options.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 8.2 Where the loss is concentrated by coverage line
text:
8.2 Where the loss is concentrated by coverage line
The total premium has eight coverage components. Decomposing the total loss ratio into per coverage loss ratios reveals which lines are profitable and which are loss making within each product.


Show the code
coverages <- tribble(
  ~coverage, ~premium_col, ~incurred_col,
  "Liability", "liability_premium", "liability_incurred",
  "Property damage", "property_damage_premium", "property_incurred",
  "Glass", "glass_premium", "glass_incurred",
  "Occupants", "occupants_premium", "occupants_incurred",
  "Fire", "fire_premium", "fire_incurred",
  "Theft", "theft_premium", "theft_incurred",
  "Legal protection", "legal_protection_premium", "legal_protection_incurred"
)

coverage_lr <- coverages |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    premium = sum(ins[[premium_col]], na.rm = TRUE),
    incurred = sum(ins[[incurred_col]], na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    loss_ratio = incurred / premium,
    coverage = fct_reorder(coverage, premium)
  )

ggplot(coverage_lr, aes(x = loss_ratio, y = coverage)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = loss_ratio > 0.7), alpha = 0.85, width = 0.65, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 0.1), "   (",
                                dollar(premium / 1e6, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 0.1),
                                "M premium)")),
            hjust = -0.05, size = 3.2, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.7, colour = "grey50", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#009E73", "TRUE" = warm)) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.35)),
                     limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Liability and property damage are the loss-making coverages",
    subtitle = "Theft, fire, legal, and occupants run comfortably under the 70 percent threshold",
    x = "Loss ratio", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_va()





Loss ratio by coverage line. Liability and property damage carry the load and run hot.










TipReading the plot



Liability premium is the largest line at 64 million EUR and runs a loss ratio of 74 percent. Property damage is the second largest at 26 million and runs 72 percent. Glass at 7 million runs 65 percent, just under threshold. The other four lines run between 40 and 52 percent and are profitable. The arithmetic is unforgiving: the two largest coverages are loss making, and the four profitable smaller coverages are not large enough to cross subsidise them. Any portfolio rebuild has to start with liability and property damage.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
8.3 Premium composition as a treemap
A treemap encodes hierarchy as nested rectangles and reads area as proportional to a numeric measure. Premium volume by policy type at the outer level and by bonus score at the inner level is a two level hierarchy that the bar charts above flatten. The treemap restores the hierarchy and colours each leaf by its loss ratio, so the structural and the performance views appear in one chart.


Show the code
tree_data <- ins |>
  group_by(policy_type, bonus_score) |>
  summarise(premium = sum(total_premium, na.rm = TRUE),
            incurred = sum(total_incurred, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(loss_ratio = incurred / premium)

ggplot(tree_data,
       aes(area = premium,
           fill = loss_ratio,
           subgroup = policy_type,
           label = paste(bonus_score, scales::percent(loss_ratio, accuracy = 1), sep = "\n"))) +
  geom_treemap(colour = "white", size = 1.5) +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_border(colour = "grey20", size = 2.5) +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_text(place = "topleft",
                             grow = FALSE, alpha = 0.7,
                             colour = "white", fontface = "bold",
                             padding.x = unit(3, "mm"),
                             padding.y = unit(3, "mm")) +
  geom_treemap_text(colour = "white", place = "centre",
                    size = 11, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_distiller(palette = "RdYlBu", direction = -1,
                       limits = c(0.4, 1.1),
                       labels = scales::percent,
                       name = "Loss ratio") +
  labs(title = "Premium volume by policy type and bonus score",
       subtitle = "Area is premium share, colour is loss ratio. Bad and neutral tiers in CC and COMP_N run hottest.")





Treemap of premium volume by policy type and bonus score. Rectangle area is proportional to premium; fill colour is loss ratio. The outer borders separate policy types; the inner labels mark bonus tiers.










TipReading the plot



The treemap immediately concentrates attention where the money sits. CC dominates the canvas at 58 percent of premium and the bad history tile inside CC is small but visibly red, indicating a loss ratio above one. COMP_E and COMP_N are the next two largest blocks, and within each of them the bad history tile is again the hottest. TPG and TP cover a small slice each and are mostly green or blue. The visual story matches the bar charts above but is faster to read, because area and colour answer two questions in one glance, how big a segment is and how it is performing. The view also reveals that the bad history red appears in every policy type, which means a uniform bad tier loading recalibration is justified rather than a product specific one.








NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



The treemapify package adds treemap geoms to the ggplot2 grammar, which means the chart composes with the rest of the report rather than requiring a separate plotting system. The area aesthetic determines rectangle size, fill determines colour, and subgroup declares the hierarchy. The two text geoms work together. geom_treemap_subgroup_text writes the policy type name onto each outer block and geom_treemap_text writes the bonus tier and loss ratio onto each inner block. The diverging RdYlBu palette is reversed so red corresponds to high loss ratios, and the limits = c(0.4, 1.1) argument fixes the colour range to the loss ratio span seen in the data and keeps the comparison meaningful across calls.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
8.4 The injury claim severity problem
Within liability, the cookbook splits claims into property damage and bodily injury. Bodily injury is what costs.


Show the code
sev_decomp <- tribble(
  ~coverage,                  ~claims_col,                ~incurred_col,
  "Liability injury",         "liability_injury_claims",  "liability_injury_incurred",
  "Liability property",       "liability_property_claims","liability_property_incurred",
  "Property damage",          "property_claims",          "property_incurred",
  "Theft",                    "theft_claims",             "theft_incurred",
  "Glass",                    "glass_claims",             "glass_incurred",
  "Fire",                     "fire_claims",              "fire_incurred",
  "Occupants",                "occupants_claims",         "occupants_incurred",
  "Legal protection",         "legal_protection_claims",  "legal_protection_incurred"
) |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    total_claims = sum(ins[[claims_col]], na.rm = TRUE),
    total_incurred = sum(ins[[incurred_col]], na.rm = TRUE),
    severity = total_incurred / total_claims
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(coverage = fct_reorder(coverage, severity))

ggplot(sev_decomp, aes(x = severity, y = coverage)) +
  geom_col(fill = warm, alpha = 0.85, width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(dollar(severity, prefix = "\u20ac", accuracy = 1),
                                "  (", comma(total_claims), " claims)")),
            hjust = -0.05, size = 3.2, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.35))) +
  labs(
    title = "Bodily injury severity dwarfs every other claim type",
    subtitle = "One bodily injury claim costs the same as seven property damage claims",
    x = "Mean incurred per claim", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_va()





Mean incurred per claim by sub-coverage, for policies with at least one claim in that sub-coverage. Bodily injury claims average roughly 5x the cost of property damage claims.










TipReading the plot



A bodily injury claim costs roughly 4,940 EUR on average, more than four times the next largest category. Property damage averages 706 EUR. Glass averages well under 200 EUR. Bodily injury is rare (around 4,800 claims out of 50,500 total claims, or 9 percent) but every one of them is structurally more expensive. The implication for pricing is that the bodily injury sub tariff needs to track external indicators of medical and legal cost inflation, because that is where most of the recent loss ratio uplift in the liability line is coming from.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: 8.5 Volatility: the coefficient of variation by segment
text:
8.5 Volatility: the coefficient of variation by segment
The coefficient of variation (the standard deviation divided by the mean of incurred per policy) is a clean measure of how risky each segment is for capital purposes. A CV of one means typical claim cost equals the average. A CV of fifteen means the typical large loss is fifteen times the average and capital is dominated by tail risk.


Show the code
cv_data <- ins |>
  group_by(policy_type, bonus_score) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    cv = sd(total_incurred) / mean(total_incurred),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n >= 200)

ggplot(cv_data, aes(x = policy_type, y = bonus_score, fill = cv)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = number(cv, accuracy = 0.1)), size = 3.2, colour = "grey15") +
  scale_fill_continuous_sequential(palette = "Reds 3", name = "CV") +
  labs(
    title = "TPG and CC under good-history cover carry the highest tail risk",
    subtitle = "CV is standard deviation of incurred divided by mean, computed per cell",
    x = NULL, y = "Bonus score"
  ) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7))





Coefficient of variation of incurred per policy by policy type and bonus score. Higher values mean more capital required against tail losses.










TipReading the plot



The pattern inverts the loss ratio result. COMP_N, which has the worst loss ratio, has the lowest CV because every policyholder claims something and the distribution of claims is denser and less skewed. TPG and CC under good history cover, by contrast, have very high CV, between 14 and 18, because the typical policy has no claim at all and the rare loss is large. The implication for capital management is that the two product lines with the lowest loss ratios are also the segments where most reserve risk sits. Profitability and volatility are different problems and demand different responses. The CC book needs reinsurance attention even where its loss ratio is acceptable.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
10.1 Profitability
The portfolio loss ratio rose from 66 percent in 2022 to 75 percent in 2024, a 12 percent relative deterioration in two years. The deterioration is broad but unevenly distributed.
The principal driver is premium softening, not claims inflation. Claim frequency rose only 3 percent over the period and severity per exposure rose only 3 percent, both essentially flat. Average premium per exposure, however, fell roughly 9 percent over the three years, from 446 EUR to 404 EUR. The arithmetic is straightforward: with incurred costs flat and premium falling, loss ratio must rise. The softening is broad based across every policy type, and the business mix shifted from heavily new business in 2022 (97 percent of policies) toward renewals in 2024 (48 percent), both consistent with competitive pressure on rates. Within that broad pattern, two specific segments deserve attention: the two largest premium lines, liability at 64 million EUR and property damage at 26 million EUR, both run loss ratios in the low seventies, and within liability the bodily injury sub line carries a mean severity of 4,940 EUR per claim, the highest in the book. The four smaller lines (theft, fire, legal protection, occupants) run 40 to 52 percent and remain profitable.
By product, comprehensive without excess (COMP_N) crossed the underwriting break even point in 2024 at 102 percent loss ratio. The mechanism is structural: removing the excess multiplies claim frequency by a factor of five compared to basic third party, and pricing only partly compensates. Bad history policies almost doubled their loss ratio from 53 percent in 2022 to 93 percent in 2024, indicating that the bonus malus loading on the riskier tiers is stale.
By driver age, the over 75 segment runs a 102 percent loss ratio despite paying below average premium, which is a clear pricing mistake on a small but identifiable segment.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
10.2 Volatility
Loss ratio measures the average outcome, but the capital the insurer must hold is driven by the dispersion around the average. Volatility, measured as the coefficient of variation of incurred per policy, inverts the loss ratio ranking. The products with the lowest loss ratios (TPG and CC under good history cover) carry coefficients of variation between 14 and 18, meaning the typical large loss is many multiples of the average. COMP_N, in contrast, has a CV of 3.6 because most policyholders claim something and the right tail is thinner.
The bad history segment is volatile and loss making, which is the worst combination. The good history CC book is profitable on average but volatile, which is a different problem requiring reinsurance attention rather than a pricing fix.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
10.3 Implications for action
Five actions emerge from the analysis. First, a portfolio wide rate review is warranted, with particular attention to liability and property damage, which together carry two thirds of premium and have absorbed the bulk of the premium softening. Second, within liability, the bodily injury sub tariff needs recalibration against recent claims, because at nearly 5,000 EUR per claim it is the highest severity sub coverage and the line most exposed when overall pricing softens. Third, COMP_N either needs a higher loading or the reintroduction of an excess to align policyholder incentives with the rest of the book. Fourth, the bonus malus loading on the neutral and bad history tiers needs a refresh using 2024 claim data, because the gap between premium loading and observed loss has widened sharply on those tiers in particular. Fifth, reinsurance and capital allocation should be calibrated against the tail rather than the average. The Lorenz analysis shows that the top 5 percent of policies generate over 80 percent of incurred costs, with a Gini coefficient of 0.96. Uniform rate increases punish the 80 percent of policies that contribute almost nothing to losses, while leaving the top decile of risk under loaded.
The methodological point is the value of separating profitability from volatility, and the value of separating the average from the tail. A single loss ratio number hides which segments are losing money on average, which segments are stable but capital intensive, and which segments are both. The Lorenz curve further shows that within any segment, the bulk of the cost concentrates in a small share of policies. Visual analytics makes those distinctions legible, and the ECDF, Lorenz curve, heatmap, ridge plot, and bubble chart in this report each show a different facet of the same underlying portfolio.

The missingness count from the previous section identified four variables affected, all under one percent. Dropping the affected rows preserves the analytical workflow without imputing.

nrow(ins)


[1] 352338


The working dataset retains 352,338 rows, which is 99.5 percent of the original.

Dropping is justifiable here because the missingness is small. If fuel_type were missing in 10 percent of rows, perhaps creating an “unknown” category would be a better choice as dropping could cause biasness to the rest of the portfolio metrics.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
Motor insurance portfolios in Europe are under pressure. The cost of bodily injury claims has been climbing, vehicle repair costs have followed a similar trajectory, and premiums in many books have not kept pace. Visual analytics offers a way to expose where the deterioration is concentrated, instead of relying only on aggregate combined ratios that hide the structure underneath.
The setting for this exercise comes from the Swiss Re sigma 4/2019 report, which observes that insurers often carry underperforming portfolios where the root causes of poor profitability and high volatility are unknown. The argument the report makes is that visual analytics can reveal underlying loss drivers and help refine segmentation and underwriting strategy.



The dataset comes from a Spanish non life insurer specialising in motor insurance and is hosted on Mendeley Data. It covers three calendar years of policy year exposures across roughly 354,000 records. Each row represents one policy in one year, with information on the policyholder, the vehicle, the coverage structure, the premiums collected for each coverage, the claim counts, the incurred amounts, and the time on risk.
The accompanying variable cookbook groups the columns into four blocks. The first block describes the policy and the insured. The second block describes the driver and the vehicle. The third block records the premium for each coverage. The fourth block records the exposure, claim counts, and incurred amounts for each coverage.



The brief is to apply univariate and bivariate visual analytics techniques to discover the distributions and characteristics of the variables, and to investigate the factors influencing the profitability and volatility of the portfolio.
Two definitions shape the analysis. Profitability in motor insurance is measured through the loss ratio, which is incurred claims divided by earned premium. A loss ratio under one means the line is profitable before expenses. Volatility refers to how much the incurred amount swings from policy to policy, captured by tail percentiles and the coefficient of variation rather than by mean alone, because the loss distribution is heavy tailed.

$ liability_exposure          <dbl> 0.09863014, 1.00000000, 1.00000000, 1.0000…


Three observations from the schema. First, the policy descriptors (policy_type, policy_status, business_type, payment_frequency, bonus_score, fuel_type, municipality_type, circulation_area) arrive as character codes and need recoding into factors before they can be used as grouping variables. Second, the premium and incurred columns appear in parallel pairs across eight coverage lines plus a total, matching the cookbook’s premiums and exposure blocks. Third, the exposure variable arrives as a numeric ranging across partial and multi year contracts, which makes per exposure aggregation the right strategy for cross policy comparisons.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery

3.2 Year coverage
The schema does not surface the range of year. A separate count confirms the span and the distribution of records across years.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
3.3 Missing values
The next check is missingness column by column, because the wrangling decisions depend on how concentrated the gaps are.


Show the code
raw |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.x)))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "missing") |>
  filter(missing > 0) |>
  mutate(pct = missing / nrow(raw)) |>
  arrange(desc(missing)) |>
  kable(caption = "Variables with missing values",
        col.names = c("Variable", "Missing rows", "Share"),
        digits = 4)

Only four variables carry any missing values, and all four affect well under one percent of rows. fuel_type has the largest gap at 1,287 rows. The next section drops the affected rows rather than imputing, because the share is small enough that complete case analysis is the cleaner choice.

The summary surfaces three points that shape later choices. First, total_incurred has a median of zero and a maximum near 580,000 EUR, which is the heavy right tail that drives the later choice to plot incurred on a log scale or to filter to claiming policies. Second, vehicle_value spans from low hundreds to over 370,000 EUR, justifying the quintile transformation used in the bivariate analysis. Third, age_driving_licence reports calendar years of licence issuance rather than years held, which the wrangling section corrects with a derived licence_tenure variable.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
Dropping rows with missing values
The missingness count from the previous section identified four variables affected, all under one percent. Dropping the affected rows preserves the analytical workflow without imputing.


Show the code
ins <- raw |>
  drop_na(fuel_type, vehicle_value, vehicle_age, age_driving_licence)

nrow(ins)


[1] 352338


The working dataset retains 352,338 rows, which is 99.5 percent of the original.






NoteThings I learnt



Dropping is justifiable here because the missingness is small and the variables affected are central to later analysis. If fuel_type were missing in 10 percent of rows, an “unknown” category would be the right move instead, because dropping would silently bias the rest of the portfolio metrics.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
Recoding categorical variables
The cookbook codes carry information but lose interpretability the moment they hit a chart. The next step is to rewrite them as ordered factors, so axes and legends read naturally.


Show the code
ins <- ins |>
  mutate(
    policy_type = factor(
      policy_type,
      levels = c("TP", "TPG", "CC", "COMP_E", "COMP_N"),
      labels = c(
        "TP\nThird party only",
        "TPG\nThird party + glass",
        "CC\nThird party + 2 covers",
        "COMP_E\nComprehensive with excess",
        "COMP_N\nComprehensive no excess"
      )
    ),
    policy_status = factor(
      policy_status,
      levels = c("A", "C"),
      labels = c("Active", "Cancelled")
    ),
    business_type = factor(
      business_type,
      levels = c("NB", "P"),
      labels = c("New business", "Portfolio renewal")
    ),
    payment_frequency = factor(
      payment_frequency,
      levels = c("A", "S", "Q"),
      labels = c("Annual", "Semiannual", "Quarterly")
    ),
    bonus_score = factor(
      bonus_score,
      levels = c("G", "N", "B"),
      labels = c("Good history", "Neutral", "Bad history")
    ),
    fuel_type = factor(
      fuel_type,
      levels = c("D", "G"),
      labels = c("Diesel", "Gasoline")
    ),
    municipality_type = factor(
      municipality_type,
      levels = c("I", "C", "IS"),
      labels = c("Inland", "Coastal", "Islands")
    ),
    circulation_area = factor(
      circulation_area,
      levels = c("U", "R"),
      labels = c("Urban", "Rural")
    ),
    year = factor(year)
  )








NoteThings I learnt



Two design choices sit behind the recoding above. The labels use newline characters so that the long descriptive label sits under a short code, keeping x axis ticks readable without rotation. The factor levels are ordered by coverage breadth, from minimal third party to comprehensive without excess, so the chart reads from least to most insurance protection rather than alphabetically.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
text:
Derived variables
Three derived variables make the analysis cleaner and more direct.
The first is licence tenure. The cookbook flags that age_driving_licence is the calendar year of licence issuance, not the number of years held. Subtracting it from the policy year gives the actual experience behind the wheel, which is the more relevant underwriting signal.


Show the code
ins <- ins |>
  mutate(
    licence_tenure = as.numeric(as.character(year)) - age_driving_licence,
    licence_tenure = pmax(licence_tenure, 0)
  )

summary(ins$licence_tenure)


   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
   1944    1998    2003    2000    2004    2024 


The pmax floor handles two records where the subtraction produced a small negative number, which would be impossible by construction. They are coded as zero years of tenure, which is the most conservative interpretation.
The second is age bins for the driver, in five year intervals between 18 and 75, plus an over 75 group. The bins follow common actuarial conventions and align cleanly with how risk is generally priced.


Show the code
ins <- ins |>
  mutate(
    age_band = cut(
      driver_age,
      breaks = c(17, 25, 35, 45, 55, 65, 75, 99),
      labels = c("18-25", "26-35", "36-45", "46-55", "56-65", "66-75", "76+"),
      ordered_result = TRUE
    )
  )


The third is vehicle value quintile, computed with ntile. Quintiles control for the heavy right skew of vehicle value, where the top percent stretches from 71,000 EUR to over 374,000 EUR.


Show the code
ins <- ins |>
  mutate(
    value_quintile = ntile(vehicle_value, 5),
    value_quintile = factor(
      value_quintile,
      levels = 1:5,
      labels = c("Q1\nLowest", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4", "Q5\nHighest")
    )
  )

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: Target variables: premium and incurred amount
text:
Target variables: premium and incurred amount
Before plotting, summary statistics establish the scale and the share of zero claim policies, which the chart then renders visually.


Show the code
target_summary <- tibble(
  Metric = c("Min", "1st quartile", "Median", "Mean", "3rd quartile", "Max"),
  `Total premium (EUR)` = c(
    min(ins$total_premium),
    quantile(ins$total_premium, 0.25),
    median(ins$total_premium),
    mean(ins$total_premium),
    quantile(ins$total_premium, 0.75),
    max(ins$total_premium)
  ),
  `Total incurred (EUR)` = c(
    min(ins$total_incurred),
    quantile(ins$total_incurred, 0.25),
    median(ins$total_incurred),
    mean(ins$total_incurred),
    quantile(ins$total_incurred, 0.75),
    max(ins$total_incurred)
  )
)

target_summary |>
  kable(digits = 0, format.args = list(big.mark = ","))





Metric
Total premium (EUR)
Total incurred (EUR)

0

146

296

3rd quartile

Show the code
zero_share <- mean(ins$total_incurred == 0)
tibble(
  `Policy years` = nrow(ins),
  `With at least one claim` = sum(ins$total_incurred > 0),
  `Zero claim share` = scales::percent(zero_share, accuracy = 0.1)
) |> kable()





Policy years
With at least one claim
Zero claim share




352338
41978
88.1%





The table confirms three points the chart below visualises. First, the premium median (258 EUR) sits well below the mean (296 EUR), indicating right skew. Second, the incurred median is zero, because the median policy year pays no claim. Third, the incurred maximum approaches 580,000 EUR. The zero claim share is 86 percent of policy years, which is why the histogram below filters to the claiming subset.


Show the code
theme_va <- function() {
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 10),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      axis.title.y = element_text(angle = 90),
      plot.background = element_rect(fill = "#fafafa", colour = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#fafafa", colour = NA)
    )
}

acc <- "#0072B2"
warm <- "#D55E00"
neutral <- "#999999"

mean_prem <- mean(ins$total_premium)
median_prem <- median(ins$total_premium)

box_prem <- ggplot(ins, aes(x = total_premium, y = "")) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = neutral, colour = "grey30", outlier.alpha = 0.05, outlier.size = 0.6) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", colour = warm, size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"), limits = c(0, 1500)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_va() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_blank())

hist_prem <- ggplot(ins, aes(x = total_premium)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = acc, alpha = 0.85, colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = median_prem, colour = "grey20", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean_prem, colour = warm, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = median_prem + 35, y = 38000,
           label = paste0("Median \u20ac", round(median_prem)),
           colour = "grey20", hjust = 0, size = 3.4) +
  annotate("text", x = mean_prem + 35, y = 34000,
           label = paste0("Mean \u20ac", round(mean_prem)),
           colour = warm, hjust = 0, size = 3.4) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"), limits = c(0, 1500)) +
  labs(
    title = "Total premium per policy year",
    subtitle = "Heavy right skew. Most policies pay under \u20ac400, a thin tail reaches above \u20ac1,500.",
    x = "Total premium",
    y = "Number of policy years"
  ) +
  theme_va()

# Incurred is more extreme: zero-inflated and heavy right tail
inc_nonzero <- ins |> filter(total_incurred > 0)
mean_inc <- mean(inc_nonzero$total_incurred)
median_inc <- median(inc_nonzero$total_incurred)

hist_inc <- ggplot(inc_nonzero, aes(x = total_incurred)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = warm, alpha = 0.85, colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = median_inc, colour = "grey20", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean_inc, colour = acc, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = median_inc + 100, y = 7500,
           label = paste0("Median \u20ac", round(median_inc)),
           colour = "grey20", hjust = 0, size = 3.4) +
  annotate("text", x = mean_inc + 100, y = 6800,
           label = paste0("Mean \u20ac", round(mean_inc)),
           colour = acc, hjust = 0, size = 3.4) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_dollar(prefix = "\u20ac"), limits = c(0, 6000)) +
  labs(
    title = "Total incurred per policy year (policies with at least one claim)",
    subtitle = paste0("Among the ",
                      scales::comma(nrow(inc_nonzero)),
                      " policy years with claims, the mean is pulled well above the median by the right tail."),
    x = "Total incurred",
    y = "Number of policy years"
  ) +
  theme_va()

(box_prem / hist_prem / hist_inc) +
  plot_layout(heights = c(0.4, 1, 1))





Distributions of total premium and total incurred amount. The boxplot above each histogram summarises the same data in a different idiom, making the right skew visible at a glance.










TipReading the plot



The premium distribution is right skewed but reasonably concentrated. The interquartile range sits between 175 EUR (Q1) and 367 EUR (Q3), with most policies paying between those values. The mean at 296 EUR sits above the median at 258 EUR, the standard pattern for a right tailed distribution. The incurred distribution is more extreme. The 86 percent of policy years that pay no claim are not plotted. Among the 14 percent that do claim, the mean is roughly 1,750 EUR but the median is under 1,000 EUR, with a tail running well past 5,000 EUR and a maximum near 580,000 EUR. Any chart of incurred amounts later in the report must respect this zero inflation and heavy tail.








NoteThings I learnt



Pairing a boxplot above a histogram is one of the cleanest ways to read a right skewed distribution. The boxplot makes the median, the IQR, and the outliers immediately legible, and the histogram fills in the shape between them. The two views share an x axis on patchwork, which is the entire reason for using patchwork here rather than facet_wrap. The two panels show the same variable in two complementary idioms, not two slices of the data.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Visual Analytics for Data Discovery
section: Driver age, vehicle age, and licence tenure
text:
Driver age, vehicle age, and licence tenure
These three numerical variables are the core underwriting inputs in motor insurance, so they get a comparative panel.


Show the code
age_summary <- ins |>
  select(`Driver age` = driver_age,
         `Vehicle age` = vehicle_age,
         `Licence tenure` = licence_tenure) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Years") |>
  group_by(Variable) |>
  summarise(
    Min = min(Years),
    Q1 = quantile(Years, 0.25),
    Median = median(Years),
    Mean = mean(Years),
    Q3 = quantile(Years, 0.75),
    Max = max(Years),
    SD = sd(Years),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

age_summary |> kable(digits = 1)





Variable

Q1
Median

Max
SD




Driver age
18

49.0
58

Licence tenure
1944
1998
2003
2000.3

2024
6.4


Vehicle age
0
17

25.9
